questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with atrial fibrillation is started on warfarin for stroke prevention. His physician needs a laboratory test to guide dose adjustments. Which of the following tests is most appropriate for monitoring the degree of anticoagulation?

explanation.summary: The INR (a standardized PT) measures the extrinsic and common pathways and is the preferred test for monitoring warfarin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Warfarin interferes with gamma-carboxylation of the vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, and X, most of which lie in the extrinsic and common pathways that the PT/INR measures. The INR standardizes the PT across laboratories, making it the best test to adjust warfarin dosing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The aPTT measures the intrinsic and common pathways and is used to monitor heparin, not warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bleeding time assesses primary hemostasis (platelets and vessel wall), not the coagulation cascade affected by warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thrombin time assesses only the fibrinogen-to-fibrin conversion and is not used to monitor warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D-dimer is a marker of fibrinolysis/thrombosis and does not reflect the degree of warfarin anticoagulation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is admitted for a pulmonary embolism and started on a continuous intravenous unfractionated heparin infusion. Which of the following laboratory tests is most appropriate to monitor her level of anticoagulation?

explanation.summary: The aPTT measures the intrinsic and common pathways and is the standard test for monitoring unfractionated heparin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heparin activates antithrombin, which then inhibits many factors including IIa, IXa, Xa, XIa, and XIIa. In practice the aPTT is most useful for monitoring the intrinsic and common pathways, so it is used to follow heparin therapy (an anti-Xa assay is an alternative).

whyWrongByChoice.A: The INR is used to monitor warfarin, which affects the extrinsic and common pathways.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The PT measures the extrinsic and common pathways and is not the standard test for heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bleeding time reflects primary hemostasis and does not track heparin effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The FDP assay measures fibrinolysis products and is not used to monitor heparin dosing.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man being evaluated for prolonged bleeding after a dental extraction is found to have a prolonged prothrombin time with a completely normal activated partial thromboplastin time. A deficiency of which of the following factors best explains this pattern?

explanation.summary: Factor VII is unique to the extrinsic pathway, which is assessed by the PT but not the aPTT.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PT measures the activity of factor VII down through fibrin formation (extrinsic and common pathways). Because factor VII is not part of the intrinsic pathway, an isolated factor VII deficiency prolongs the PT while leaving the aPTT normal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor VIII is an intrinsic pathway factor; its deficiency prolongs the aPTT, not the PT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor IX is intrinsic; deficiency prolongs the aPTT rather than the PT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factor XI is intrinsic; deficiency prolongs the aPTT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factor XII is intrinsic; deficiency prolongs the aPTT (though it causes no clinical bleeding).

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy has a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time and a normal prothrombin time. When his plasma is mixed 1:1 with normal plasma, the aPTT corrects into the normal range both immediately and after incubation. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: Correction of a prolonged aPTT with normal plasma indicates a clotting factor deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a mixing study, if the aPTT corrects after adding normal plasma, the missing ingredient was a coagulation factor supplied by the normal plasma. A boy with an isolated prolonged aPTT that corrects most likely has hemophilia (factor VIII deficiency), which can then be confirmed with a specific factor assay.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lupus anticoagulant does not correct on mixing because it is an inhibitor, not a deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Heparin would not correct on mixing, since the drug remains in the mixture and blocks the reagent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A specific factor inhibitor typically corrects immediately but becomes prolonged again after incubation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DIC produces prolonged PT and aPTT with low platelets and elevated D-dimer, not an isolated correcting aPTT.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman has a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time. A mixing study with normal plasma fails to correct the aPTT both immediately and after incubation. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: A prolonged aPTT that fails to correct on mixing, both immediately and after incubation, indicates a lupus anticoagulant.
explanation.whyCorrect: Failure to correct means a circulating inhibitor, not a missing factor, is present. A lupus anticoagulant (an antiphospholipid antibody) binds the phospholipid in the reagent and keeps the aPTT prolonged immediately and after incubation, matching the Case #3 mixing-study pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hemophilia A is a factor deficiency and would correct on mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor VII deficiency prolongs the PT, not the aPTT, and is a deficiency that corrects on mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K deficiency reduces factors II, VII, IX, X (a deficiency) and would correct on mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand disease chiefly affects platelet adhesion and factor VIII carriage and corrects on mixing.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus develops a deep venous thrombosis and is found to have a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time that does not correct on a mixing study; testing confirms a lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following best explains why her aPTT is prolonged despite her tendency to clot?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consumption of clotting factors by widespread microthrombi

choices.label: B
choices.text: The antibody binds and inactivates phospholipid in the assay reagent

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hereditary deficiency of factor VIII

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired gamma-carboxylation from vitamin K deficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased hepatic synthesis of all coagulation factors

explanation.summary: Antiphospholipid antibodies prolong the aPTT in vitro as a lab artifact while promoting thrombosis in vivo.
explanation.whyCorrect: In vitro, antiphospholipid antibodies/lupus anticoagulant bind to and inactivate phospholipid-containing reagents used in the aPTT, leaving less reagent available and falsely prolonging the aPTT. This is a pure laboratory artifact; in vivo these antibodies actually increase thrombosis, putting patients at risk for DVT and pulmonary embolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Consumption of factors describes DIC, not the mechanism of a lupus anticoagulant, and would not fail to correct as an isolated finding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A factor VIII deficiency would correct on mixing and does not cause thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K deficiency reduces functional factors and prolongs the PT; it does not explain a non-correcting aPTT with thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Impaired hepatic synthesis is a factor deficiency that corrects on mixing and causes bleeding, not thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man scheduled for elective hernia repair is found on routine testing to have an isolated prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time. He has no personal or family history of abnormal bleeding and has previously undergone surgery without bleeding complications. A deficiency of which of the following factors best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Factor XII deficiency prolongs the aPTT in vitro but does not cause clinical bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Factor XII deficiency prolongs clotting time in vitro (prolonged aPTT) because factor XII participates in the contact-activated intrinsic pathway of the assay. However, factor XII is not involved in physiologic coagulation, so its deficiency does not cause clinical bleeding, explaining this asymptomatic patient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factor VIII deficiency (hemophilia A) prolongs the aPTT and causes significant clinical bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor IX deficiency (hemophilia B) prolongs the aPTT and causes clinical bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor XI deficiency prolongs the aPTT and can cause bleeding, unlike the asymptomatic picture here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand disease causes mucocutaneous bleeding and typically a bleeding history, not an asymptomatic isolated aPTT prolongation.

questions.stem: A patient in the intensive care unit suddenly begins to ooze blood from his central venous line and surgical sites. Disseminated intravascular coagulation is suspected. Which of the following sets of laboratory findings would most support this diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated platelet count with normal PT

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prolonged PT and aPTT with an elevated D-dimer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal PT, normal aPTT, and normal D-dimer

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isolated low D-dimer with normal platelets

choices.label: E
choices.text: Isolated prolonged thrombin time with normal PT and aPTT

explanation.summary: DIC consumes clotting factors and platelets while generating clot breakdown, giving prolonged PT/aPTT with elevated D-dimer.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DIC, abnormal activation of the coagulation cascade produces widespread microthrombi and rapid consumption of clotting factors (prolonging PT/INR and aPTT), fibrin, and platelets (causing thrombocytopenia). Ongoing fibrinolysis of those clots raises the D-dimer, so prolonged PT and aPTT with an elevated D-dimer best supports DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DIC causes thrombocytopenia (low platelets) and a prolonged PT, not an elevated platelet count with a normal PT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Entirely normal studies would argue against DIC, which produces multiple abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DIC raises the D-dimer and lowers platelets, opposite to this choice.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DIC produces global abnormalities, not an isolated prolonged thrombin time with otherwise normal tests.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with chest pain. Her vital signs, physical examination, and electrocardiogram are unremarkable, and clinical scoring places her at low probability for pulmonary embolism. Which of the following tests is the most appropriate and cost-effective choice to rule out a pulmonary embolism?

explanation.summary: A negative highly sensitive D-dimer in a low-probability patient effectively rules out pulmonary embolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: The D-dimer assay is very sensitive and is almost always elevated when a thrombus is present, making it excellent for ruling out clot. A negative result with such a sensitive test in a low-probability scenario effectively rules out pulmonary embolism, avoiding unnecessary imaging and cost.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT angiography is used to rule in a thrombus and is unnecessary imaging in a low-probability patient before a D-dimer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombin time assesses fibrinogen-to-fibrin conversion and does not rule out pulmonary embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bleeding time measures primary hemostasis and is unrelated to detecting a thrombus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The INR monitors extrinsic/common pathway activity (e.g., warfarin) and cannot exclude a pulmonary embolism.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with chronic alcohol use disorder arrives intoxicated with a protuberant abdomen after weeks of continuous drinking. Laboratory studies show a prolonged prothrombin time, prolonged partial thromboplastin time, and thrombocytopenia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the prolonged PT and PTT?

explanation.summary: The liver produces all coagulation factors, so advanced liver disease prolongs all coagulation assays.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient's chronic alcohol use and protuberant abdomen point to cirrhosis. Because all coagulation factors are produced in the liver, advanced liver disease decreases them all, prolonging the PT, PTT, and TT. Liver disease is the most common acquired cause of coagulation abnormalities.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An antibody against factor VII would prolong the PT but not the PTT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic kidney disease causes uremic platelet dysfunction but does not directly reduce clotting factors to prolong PT and PTT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Isolated factor VII deficiency prolongs the PT only, not the PTT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The clinical picture fits cirrhosis; the patient is not receiving regular heparin injections.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a lifelong history of easy bruising is found to have a prolonged thrombin time despite a normal prothrombin time and normal activated partial thromboplastin time. The thrombin time most directly assesses which of the following steps?

explanation.summary: The thrombin time isolates the final step of the common pathway: fibrinogen-to-fibrin conversion.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thrombin time is performed by adding thrombin to plasma and measuring the time to form fibrin, so it assesses conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Because it sits at the very end of the common pathway, it is a specialized test most often used to detect fibrinogen deficiency or a fibrinogen functional mutation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The extrinsic pathway is assessed by the PT, not the thrombin time.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The intrinsic pathway is assessed by the aPTT, not the thrombin time.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet plug formation is primary hemostasis, evaluated by platelet tests, not the thrombin time.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin K-dependent carboxylation relates to factor synthesis and is reflected in the PT/INR, not the thrombin time.

questions.stem: A laboratory technician draws blood for coagulation testing into a light-blue-top vacutainer tube that contains citrate. Which of the following best explains why citrate is required for the sample to be usable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides a phospholipid surface to accelerate clotting

choices.label: B
choices.text: It removes calcium to prevent the blood from clotting before testing

choices.label: C
choices.text: It adds thrombin to standardize fibrin formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: It activates the intrinsic pathway for the assay

choices.label: E
choices.text: It supplies tissue factor needed for the prothrombin time

explanation.summary: Citrate chelates calcium, preventing clotting in the tube so timed coagulation tests can be run.
explanation.whyCorrect: The light-blue-top tube contains citrate, which removes calcium from the blood. Because calcium binds the phospholipids exposed during platelet activation and provides a surface for assembly of some coagulation factors, removing it prevents clotting in the tube. Calcium is then added back during the actual assay so the timed test can be performed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phospholipid and calcium are added later during the assay; citrate does not provide a clotting surface.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombin is added only in the specific thrombin time assay, not by the collection tube.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intrinsic pathway activators (silica, kaolin) are reagents used in the aPTT, not the anticoagulant in the tube.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tissue factor (thromboplastin) is a PT reagent added in the lab, not the citrate in the collection tube.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with acute onset of right-sided weakness and slurred speech. Imaging confirms an acute ischemic stroke, and he is given a thrombolytic agent that restores blood flow by breaking down the occluding clot. Which of the following molecules is directly responsible for dissolving the already-formed clot?

explanation.summary: Plasmin is the active enzyme of fibrinolysis that degrades fibrin and dissolves clots.
explanation.whyCorrect: Clots are broken down through fibrinolysis, and the main actor is the enzyme plasmin, which cleaves the fibrin surrounding a clot into small fragments that wash away. Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) generates plasmin from plasminogen and is used clinically to dissolve clots after strokes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antithrombin stops the formation of new fibrin by inhibiting thrombin, but it does not dissolve fibrin that has already formed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrinogen is the inactive precursor that is converted into fibrin to build a clot, not to break one down.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasminogen is the inactive zymogen precursor; it must be activated to plasmin before it can degrade fibrin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombin promotes clotting by converting fibrinogen to fibrin, the opposite of clot dissolution.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old woman reports frequent nosebleeds, easy bruising, heavy menstrual periods, and gum bleeding when she brushes her teeth. Her platelet count, PT, and aPTT are near normal, and specialized testing shows reduced activity of a protein secreted by endothelial cells. Which of the following best describes the normal function of this protein in primary hemostasis?

explanation.summary: Von Willebrand factor acts as a molecular glue that mediates platelet adhesion to the subendothelium.
explanation.whyCorrect: Von Willebrand factor is secreted by endothelial cells and binds both exposed subendothelial collagen and platelet glycoprotein Ib receptors, anchoring platelets to the damaged wall during platelet adhesion. Its deficiency causes von Willebrand disease, the most common hereditary bleeding disorder, with mucocutaneous bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Prothrombin is activated to thrombin by activated Factor X in the common pathway, not by von Willebrand factor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cross-linking of fibrin is performed by Factor XIII during secondary hemostasis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dissolving fibrin is the job of plasmin during fibrinolysis, not von Willebrand factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin is catalyzed by thrombin, part of secondary hemostasis.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation is started on warfarin, an anticoagulant that blocks the recycling of vitamin K. Which of the following best describes the role of vitamin K in normal coagulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It binds calcium ions directly to form a clot

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a cofactor for gamma-carboxylation of Factors II, VII, IX, and X

choices.label: D
choices.text: It directly cleaves prothrombin into thrombin

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is required for the synthesis of fibrinogen

explanation.summary: Vitamin K enables gamma-carboxylation of glutamate residues on Factors II, VII, IX, and X so they can bind calcium.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vitamin K is a cofactor in the gamma-carboxylation of glutamate residues on clotting Factors II, VII, IX, and X (and proteins C and S), a modification that lets these factors bind calcium and become functional. Warfarin inhibits the enzyme that recycles vitamin K, reducing functional levels of these factors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vitamin K does not bind calcium itself; it enables the clotting factors to bind calcium after they are carboxylated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Plasminogen is converted to plasmin by tPA, not by vitamin K.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prothrombin is cleaved to thrombin by activated Factor X in the common pathway, not by vitamin K.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibrinogen synthesis occurs in the liver and is not vitamin K dependent.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is evaluated for a swollen, painful knee that developed after minimal activity. He has a history of prolonged bleeding after a tooth extraction. Laboratory testing shows a prolonged aPTT with a normal PT and normal platelet count. A deficiency of which of the following factors most likely explains his presentation?

explanation.summary: Hemophilia A is an X-linked Factor VIII deficiency affecting the intrinsic pathway, causing hemarthroses with prolonged aPTT and normal PT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemophilia A is caused by Factor VIII deficiency, an X-linked recessive disorder that mainly affects males and presents with muscle, joint, and deep tissue bleeding such as hemarthrosis. Because Factor VIII is part of the intrinsic pathway, the aPTT is prolonged while the PT (extrinsic pathway) remains normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factor V is part of the common pathway, and its involvement would prolong both PT and aPTT; it is better known for the Factor V Leiden thrombophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor VII deficiency affects the extrinsic pathway and would prolong the PT, not selectively the aPTT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factor XIII cross-links fibrin; its deficiency does not prolong the aPTT because standard tests still form an initial clot.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand disease usually has a normal or only mildly prolonged aPTT and features mucocutaneous rather than deep joint bleeding.

questions.stem: Immediately after a small arteriole is lacerated, a series of events begins to limit blood loss. Which of the following is the first event in primary hemostasis?

explanation.summary: Vessel constriction is the first step of primary hemostasis, reducing blood flow to the injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Almost immediately after a vessel is damaged, regional small arterioles constrict, driven by endothelin released from endothelial cells. This vasoconstriction reduces blood loss and brings platelets and clotting factors into closer contact with the injured wall before the plug forms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thrombin activation occurs during secondary hemostasis, well after the initial vascular response.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibrin formation stabilizes the plug during secondary hemostasis, not at the outset.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet adhesion occurs after constriction, once platelets contact exposed collagen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet aggregation is a later step that forms the soft platelet plug.

questions.stem: A patient sustains a deep laceration, and clotting factors in the blood are exposed to a substance released from the damaged tissue that sits at the top of the extrinsic pathway. Which of the following substances triggers the extrinsic pathway of the coagulation cascade?

explanation.summary: Tissue factor (Factor III) released by damaged tissue initiates the extrinsic pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: The extrinsic pathway is triggered by tissue factor (TF, Factor III), which is normally hidden from the bloodstream until vessel cells are damaged. Once released, it activates Factor VII and initiates coagulation along the extrinsic arm, which then proceeds through the common pathway starting at Factor X.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Exposed collagen and other negatively charged surfaces activate the intrinsic pathway, not the extrinsic pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor XII begins the intrinsic pathway in vitro and, notably, is not required for physiologic clotting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombin acts within the cascade to amplify clotting and loops back to the intrinsic pathway; it does not initiate the extrinsic arm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand factor functions in platelet adhesion during primary hemostasis, not in triggering the extrinsic pathway.

questions.stem: In the coagulation cascade, the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways converge at a single point that begins the common pathway. Which of the following factors is the convergence point where both pathways meet?

explanation.summary: Factor X is the convergence point of the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways and starts the common pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways activate Factor X to Xa, which is the start of the common pathway. From there, the common pathway (Factors X, V, II, and I) generates thrombin, which converts fibrinogen to fibrin to form a stable clot.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factor VII belongs to the extrinsic pathway only and acts upstream of the convergence point.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor VIII is part of the intrinsic pathway and does not represent the point of convergence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor IX is an intrinsic pathway factor upstream of Factor X.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factor XII initiates the intrinsic pathway in vitro and is not the shared convergence point.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman admitted with a deep vein thrombosis is started on heparin. Heparin produces its anticoagulant effect by enhancing the activity of which of the following naturally occurring molecules?

explanation.summary: Heparin enhances antithrombin, which inhibits thrombin and amplification of the cascade.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antithrombin is a natural inhibitor of thrombin, and its activity is enhanced by heparin. Because thrombin amplifies the clotting cascade, boosting antithrombin damps both the intrinsic and common pathways. Heparin-like molecules on endothelial cells naturally augment antithrombin at the vessel wall.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Plasmin dissolves existing clots during fibrinolysis and is not the target enhanced by heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Protein C is activated by the thrombin-thrombomodulin complex with protein S, a pathway independent of heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tissue factor pathway inhibitor switches off the extrinsic arm but is not potentiated by heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand factor promotes platelet adhesion and is unrelated to heparin's mechanism.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman of European descent has had two unprovoked deep vein thromboses. Workup reveals a mutation in Factor V that renders it resistant to cleavage by activated protein C. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden is a mutation making Factor V resistant to activated protein C, the most common inherited thrombophilia in Caucasians.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Factor V Leiden, mutated Factor V is not degraded by activated protein C, so the anticoagulant brake fails and clotting continues unchecked. It is the most common inherited thrombophilia (hypercoagulability) in Caucasian populations of European descent and predisposes to venous thromboses.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemophilia A is a Factor VIII deficiency that causes bleeding, not thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Liver failure reduces clotting factor synthesis and tends to cause bleeding rather than clotting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K deficiency impairs Factors II, VII, IX, and X and causes bleeding, not a hypercoagulable state.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand disease is a bleeding disorder from deficient von Willebrand factor, the opposite of a clotting tendency.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man taking warfarin needs periodic monitoring to keep his dose in the correct range. Which laboratory test, which primarily assesses the extrinsic and common pathways, is most appropriate for monitoring his warfarin therapy?

explanation.summary: The PT/INR assesses the extrinsic and common pathways and is used to monitor warfarin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The prothrombin time assesses the extrinsic pathway and, by extension, the common pathway (Factors I, II, V, VII, and X). The INR standardizes the PT so clotting times can be compared between labs, making PT/INR the standard test to adjust warfarin dosing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The aPTT assesses the intrinsic and common pathways and is used to monitor heparin rather than warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bleeding time reflects platelet function and vessel response, not the vitamin K-dependent factors affected by warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: D-dimer is a marker of fibrin breakdown used to evaluate thrombosis, not to monitor anticoagulant dosing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet count measures platelet number and is unaffected by warfarin's action on clotting factors.

questions.stem: A healthy 30-year-old man undergoing preoperative screening is found to have an isolated prolonged aPTT with a normal PT. He has never had abnormal bleeding, even after prior dental extractions, and further testing reveals a deficiency of Factor XII (Hageman factor). Which of the following best explains why he does not have a clinical bleeding tendency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Factor XII is not involved in physiologic in vivo coagulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Factor XII deficiency is compensated by increased platelet counts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Factor XII is only needed for fibrinolysis, not clotting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Factor XII is replaced by vitamin K in the liver

choices.label: E
choices.text: Factor XII activates plasmin to prevent bleeding

explanation.summary: Factor XII deficiency prolongs the aPTT in vitro but does not cause bleeding because Factor XII is not needed for physiologic clotting.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although Factor XII deficiency prolongs clotting time in vitro (a longer aPTT), it does not cause clinical bleeding because Factor XII is not involved in physiologic coagulation. In the body, the tissue factor pathway generates thrombin that loops back to amplify the intrinsic pathway, so the cascade proceeds without Factor XII.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bleeding protection here is not due to platelet numbers; Factor XII deficiency simply lacks physiologic relevance to clotting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor XII is classically an intrinsic-pathway (clotting) factor in vitro, not a dedicated fibrinolytic factor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K supports carboxylation of Factors II, VII, IX, and X and does not replace Factor XII.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factor XII does not activate plasmin to prevent bleeding; its absence is tolerated because it is dispensable in vivo.

questions.stem: A blood bank collects a unit of whole blood into a tube containing citrate to keep it from clotting during storage. Citrate prevents coagulation by which of the following mechanisms?

explanation.summary: Citrate and EDTA block clotting by chelating calcium, which is required at multiple steps of the cascade.
explanation.whyCorrect: Calcium is required at numerous points in the coagulation cascade to bind and activate clotting factors. Chelating calcium with citrate or EDTA blocks these steps, which is why citrate is used in blood banking and blood is collected in EDTA tubes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antithrombin is potentiated by heparin, not by citrate, and this is not how citrate anticoagulates a specimen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cleaving fibrin into D-dimers is the action of plasmin during fibrinolysis, unrelated to citrate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting vitamin K recycling is the mechanism of warfarin, not citrate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Converting plasminogen to plasmin is done by tissue plasminogen activator, not by citrate.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old woman in the ICU is receiving a transfusion of packed red blood cells. A few minutes into the transfusion she develops chills, a temperature of 102.4 F, back pain, and dark red urine. Her blood pressure falls to 76/52 mm Hg. The blood bank later determines that her unit was mislabeled. Which of the following best explains her reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recipient antibodies against ABO antigens on the donor red blood cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Donor T lymphocytes attacking the recipient's tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recipient antibodies against HLA antigens on donor leukocytes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Type 1 hypersensitivity to allergens in the donor plasma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bacterial contamination of the donor unit

explanation.summary: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are caused by recipient anti-ABO antibodies binding mismatched donor RBCs.
explanation.whyCorrect: A mislabeled unit leading to ABO-incompatible transfusion causes an acute hemolytic reaction. Recipient antibodies against ABO antigens on the donor RBCs trigger fever/chills (the most common presenting symptom), back pain, hemoglobinuria, kidney failure, DIC, and hypotension/shock. The direct antiglobulin (DAT) test is positive in the post-transfusion sample.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Donor T lymphocytes attacking host tissue causes transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease, which presents with fever, rash, diarrhea, and hepatomegaly 7-10 days later, not within minutes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antibodies against donor leukocyte HLA antigens cause a febrile nonhemolytic reaction, which presents with fever and flushing without hemoglobinuria or shock.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A type 1 hypersensitivity (allergic) reaction presents with urticaria, itching, and wheezing rather than back pain and hemoglobinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bacterial contamination can cause septic shock, but the mislabeled ABO-incompatible unit and hemoglobinuria point specifically to an acute hemolytic reaction.

questions.stem: A trauma patient with unknown blood type is exsanguinating, and there is no time to complete blood typing. The blood bank issues emergency red blood cells that can be given to any recipient. Which of the following blood types are these units?

explanation.summary: O-negative is the universal red cell donor because its RBCs carry no A, B, or D antigens.
explanation.whyCorrect: The universal RBC donor is type O-negative. Because there are no A, B, or D antigens on the surface of these red cells, they will not prompt an anti-A, anti-B, or anti-D response in any recipient. Emergency blood release therefore typically uses O-negative units.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AB-positive is the universal recipient (can receive any blood), not the universal donor; its RBCs carry A, B, and D antigens.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AB-negative RBCs carry A and B antigens, which would react with anti-A and anti-B in most recipients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A-negative RBCs carry the A antigen, so they would be attacked by anti-A antibodies in type B and type O recipients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: O-positive RBCs carry the D antigen and could sensitize or react in Rh-negative recipients, so they are not truly universal.

questions.stem: A patient is described as a "universal recipient" who can safely receive red blood cells of any ABO and Rh type. Which of the following is this patient's blood type?

explanation.summary: AB-positive patients have no anti-A, anti-B, or anti-D antibodies, so they can receive any blood type.
explanation.whyCorrect: An AB-positive patient carries the A, B, and D antigens on their RBCs and therefore makes no antibodies against these antigens. With no anti-A, anti-B, or anti-D antibodies in their plasma, they can receive any type of blood, making them the universal recipient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: O-negative patients have anti-A, anti-B, and (if exposed) anti-D antibodies, so they can receive only O-negative blood.
whyWrongByChoice.B: O-positive patients have anti-A and anti-B antibodies, limiting them to O-positive or O-negative blood.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A-positive patients have anti-B antibodies and cannot receive B or AB blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: B-negative patients have anti-A (and possibly anti-D) antibodies and cannot receive A, AB, or Rh-positive blood.

questions.stem: A blood bank technologist notes that a particular patient's plasma may contain both anti-A antibodies and anti-Rh (anti-D) antibodies. Which of the following blood types is most consistent with this finding?

explanation.summary: A B-negative person lacks the A antigen and the D antigen, so they can form anti-A (naturally) and anti-D (after exposure).
explanation.whyCorrect: B-negative red cells carry only the B antigen (no A, no D). Anti-A antibodies are naturally occurring in anyone who lacks the A antigen. Anti-Rh (anti-D) antibodies are not naturally occurring but can develop after an Rh-negative person is exposed to Rh-positive blood. A B-negative patient can therefore have both anti-A and anti-D antibodies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An A-positive patient has the A and D antigens, so they make neither anti-A nor anti-D antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An A-negative patient has the A antigen and would not make anti-A; they could make anti-D only if exposed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A B-positive patient has the D antigen and would not make anti-D antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An AB-positive patient has A, B, and D antigens and makes no ABO or anti-D antibodies at all.

questions.stem: An Rh-negative woman is pregnant with her first child. Her physician plans to give an injection of anti-D antibody (RhoGAM) during pregnancy and again immediately after delivery. Which of the following best describes how this injection prevents harm in future pregnancies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It replaces the fetal red cells destroyed by maternal antibodies

choices.label: B
choices.text: It binds fetal Rh-positive red cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: It converts the mother's blood type to Rh-positive

choices.label: D
choices.text: It crosses the placenta to protect the fetal red cells directly

choices.label: E
choices.text: It suppresses production of naturally occurring anti-A and anti-B antibodies

explanation.summary: RhoGAM is an anti-D antibody that masks fetal Rh-positive cells so the mother never becomes sensitized to the D antigen.
explanation.whyCorrect: If fetal Rh-positive RBCs enter the Rh-negative mother's circulation, the RhoGAM anti-D antibodies bind those cells and prevent the mother's immune system from "seeing" the Rh antigen. This blocks formation of maternal anti-D antibodies. It is given both during pregnancy and immediately after delivery because fetal blood may enter maternal circulation at any time.

whyWrongByChoice.A: RhoGAM does not replace red cells; it prevents maternal sensitization to the D antigen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RhoGAM does not change the mother's blood type; she remains Rh-negative.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RhoGAM (like the anti-D antibody used) does not cross the placenta; that property is what makes it safe.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RhoGAM targets the D (Rh) antigen only and has no effect on naturally occurring anti-A and anti-B antibodies.

questions.stem: An Rh-negative woman with an Rh-positive fetus received no anti-D prophylaxis during her first pregnancy, which was uncomplicated. During a later pregnancy with a second Rh-positive fetus, the fetus develops severe hemolytic anemia, jaundice, and hepatomegaly. Which of the following best explains why the first fetus was spared but the second was affected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mother's initial anti-Rh antibodies were IgM and too large to cross the placenta

choices.label: B
choices.text: The first fetus was Rh-negative and the second was Rh-positive

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anti-Rh antibodies are naturally occurring and take years to reach high levels

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mother developed anti-A antibodies rather than anti-Rh antibodies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fetal red cells only enter maternal circulation during a second pregnancy

explanation.summary: In hemolytic disease of the newborn, the first-pregnancy anti-Rh response is IgM (cannot cross placenta), while later IgG antibodies do cross and attack the fetus.
explanation.whyCorrect: When fetal Rh-positive cells first sensitize the Rh-negative mother, the initial anti-Rh antibodies are IgM, which are too big to cross the placenta, so there is usually no harm in the current pregnancy. Over time these antibodies become IgG, which can cross the placenta. In a subsequent pregnancy with an Rh-positive fetus, maternal IgG anti-Rh antibodies cross and hemolyze the fetal red cells, causing hemolytic disease of the newborn.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The stem specifies both fetuses were Rh-positive, so a change in fetal Rh type does not explain the difference.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anti-Rh antibodies are not naturally occurring; they form only after exposure to Rh-positive blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The disease is caused by anti-Rh (anti-D) antibodies, not anti-A antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fetal red cells can enter maternal circulation during the first delivery (and sometimes pregnancy), which is what sensitizes the mother.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding is being prepared for an urgent transfusion. As part of pre-transfusion testing, his serum is combined with the actual red blood cells from the unit to be transfused, and the sample is observed for agglutination or hemolysis. Which of the following steps is being performed?

explanation.summary: The cross-match is the only pre-transfusion step that mixes recipient serum with the actual donor red cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cross-matching is like a mini-transfusion: the patient's serum is combined with the specific donor RBCs. If agglutination or hemolysis occurs, the patient has an antibody against an antigen on those donor cells and the unit must not be transfused. It is the only step that uses the actual donated red cells and is done immediately before transfusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Forward typing uses the patient's own RBCs and known anti-A/anti-B reagents, not donor cells.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The antibody screen tests patient serum against a manufactured panel of reagent RBCs, not the specific donor unit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reverse typing tests patient serum against known type A and type B reagent cells to confirm ABO type.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rh typing detects the D antigen on the patient's own red cells using anti-D reagent.

questions.stem: A patient with active bleeding is found to have a very low fibrinogen level (fibrinogen < 100 mg/dL). Which of the following blood products is the most appropriate choice to correct this deficiency?

explanation.summary: Cryoprecipitate is the concentrated source of fibrinogen and is preferred for bleeding with low fibrinogen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cryoprecipitate contains fibrinogen, factor VIII, and von Willebrand factor and is indicated for bleeding emergencies in patients with fibrinogen deficiency (fibrinogen < 100). It is made by thawing fresh frozen plasma and collecting the precipitate, concentrating the fibrinogen.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fresh frozen plasma does contain fibrinogen, but it is diluted in a large volume of plasma, making it less efficient for correcting isolated low fibrinogen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Granulocytes are used for uncontrolled infection with a very low neutrophil count, not for bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Packed RBCs are used for severe symptomatic anemia and do not contain clotting factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelets treat bleeding due to thrombocytopenia and would not correct low fibrinogen.

questions.stem: A patient with symptomatic anemia and a hemoglobin of 6.5 g/dL is transfused one unit of packed red blood cells. Which of the following best describes the expected effect of this single unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It raises the hemoglobin by approximately 1 g/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: It raises the platelet count by 30,000-50,000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: It replaces all of the patient's coagulation factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: It corrects a low fibrinogen level

choices.label: E
choices.text: It provides white blood cells to fight infection

explanation.summary: Packed RBCs are given for severe symptomatic anemia; one unit raises hemoglobin by roughly 1 g/dL.
explanation.whyCorrect: Packed red blood cells are indicated for severe anemia (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL, or < 8 g/dL with symptoms) to increase the oxygen-carrying capacity of blood. One unit of packed RBCs raises the hemoglobin by approximately 1 g/dL.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Raising the platelet count by 30,000-50,000/mm3 describes a unit of apheresis platelets, not packed RBCs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fresh frozen plasma, not packed RBCs, contains the coagulation factors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Correcting low fibrinogen is the role of cryoprecipitate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Granulocyte transfusions provide white blood cells; packed RBCs contain concentrated red cells.

questions.stem: Several minutes after starting a transfusion of packed red blood cells, a patient becomes hypotensive and short of breath and develops hives all over her body. She does not have a fever. Which of the following types of transfusion reaction is she most likely experiencing?

explanation.summary: An allergic (IgE-mediated) transfusion reaction presents with urticaria, wheezing, and hypotension without fever.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient is experiencing difficulty breathing and hypotension from mast cell and histamine activation, causing vasodilation and urticaria, all consistent with an anaphylactic (allergic, type 1 hypersensitivity) reaction to allergens in the donor blood. Dyspnea plus hives plus hypotension points to an IgE-mediated reaction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute hemolytic reactions present with fever, back pain, and hemoglobinuria, not urticaria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Delayed hemolytic reactions occur roughly a week or more after transfusion, not within minutes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Febrile nonhemolytic reactions present with fever and flushing, and this patient has no fever.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transfusion-related acute lung injury causes hypoxia and respiratory failure but not the hives seen here, and the dyspnea-plus-hives-plus-hypotension picture is classic for an IgE-mediated reaction.

questions.stem: A severely immunocompromised transplant recipient receives a non-irradiated blood transfusion. About 8 days later he develops fever, a diffuse rash, watery diarrhea, and hepatomegaly, and his condition rapidly deteriorates. Which of the following would most likely have prevented this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irradiation of the blood product before transfusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leukoreduction to remove donor leukocytes to reduce febrile reactions

explanation.summary: Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease is caused by donor T lymphocytes and is prevented by irradiating blood products.
explanation.whyCorrect: The fever, rash, diarrhea, and hepatomegaly appearing 7-10 days after transfusion in an immunocompromised patient describe transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease, in which donor T lymphocytes attack the host's tissues. It is 80-90% fatal, and prevention is achieved by irradiating the blood product to disable donor lymphocytes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Leukoreduction reduces febrile reactions and CMV transmission but does not reliably prevent graft-versus-host disease, which requires irradiation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antihistamines are used for allergic reactions, not graft-versus-host disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Using O-negative cells addresses ABO/Rh compatibility, not donor lymphocyte-mediated disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antipyretics treat fever symptomatically and do not prevent graft-versus-host disease.

questions.stem: A patient receiving a red blood cell transfusion develops fever and flushing but no hypotension, back pain, hemoglobinuria, or urticaria. The reaction is attributed to recipient antibodies directed against HLA antigens on donor leukocytes. Which of the following interventions would most reduce the incidence of this type of reaction in the future?

explanation.summary: Febrile nonhemolytic reactions result from antibodies against donor leukocytes and are reduced by leukoreduction.
explanation.whyCorrect: A febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction is caused by recipient antibodies against HLA antigens on donor leukocytes and presents with fever and flushing. Removing the white blood cells from the product (leukoreduction) reduces the incidence of these reactions; antipyretic premedication may also be used.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Irradiation prevents transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease, not febrile nonhemolytic reactions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: O-negative units address ABO/Rh compatibility and do not target leukocyte-related febrile reactions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epinephrine is used to treat anaphylaxis from allergic reactions, not to prevent febrile reactions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cryoprecipitate is a specific product for fibrinogen deficiency and is unrelated to preventing febrile reactions.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for a persistent nosebleed. He is adopted and his family history is unknown. On examination he has diffuse ecchymoses. Laboratory studies show a normal platelet count, a normal PT/INR, and a prolonged PTT that corrects when normal plasma is added to the sample. A specific factor assay returns a low factor VIII activity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A prolonged PTT that corrects with mixing plus a low factor VIII assay identifies hemophilia A.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemophilia A results from a defect in factor VIII, part of the intrinsic arm of the coagulation cascade, so the PTT is prolonged while the PT is normal. Because the defect is a missing factor rather than an inhibitor, adding normal plasma restores the factor and corrects the PTT. A specific factor assay showing low factor VIII activity confirms hemophilia A and distinguishes it from hemophilia B.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemophilia B is caused by a factor IX defect; the specific factor assay here showed low factor VIII, not factor IX.
whyWrongByChoice.C: von Willebrand disease impairs platelet plug formation and is associated with petechiae, which hemophilia patients characteristically lack.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemophilia C is an extremely rare factor XI disorder; the low factor VIII assay points to hemophilia A.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immune thrombocytopenia would produce a low platelet count, but this patient's platelet count is normal.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying the intrinsic arm of the coagulation cascade to understand the pathophysiology of hemophilia. In this pathway, activated factor VIII (VIIIa) exerts its primary hemostatic effect through which of the following actions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serving as a cofactor for activated factor IX to activate factor X

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activating the extrinsic pathway via tissue factor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anchoring platelets to damaged blood vessels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Converting prothrombin to thrombin independently of factor X

explanation.summary: Factor VIIIa is a cofactor for factor IXa, and together they activate factor X to start the common pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the intrinsic arm, activated factor VIII (VIIIa) serves as a cofactor for activated factor IX (IXa). They cooperate to activate factor X to Xa at the start of the common pathway, which eventually leads to fibrin formation that stabilizes the clot. A defect in factor VIII (or factor IX) therefore impairs fibrin formation and causes excessive bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrinogen is cleaved to fibrin by thrombin downstream in the common pathway, not by factor VIIIa.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The extrinsic pathway is initiated by tissue factor and factor VIIa and is unaffected in hemophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anchoring platelets to damaged vessels is the role of von Willebrand factor, not factor VIIIa.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prothrombin is converted to thrombin by factor Xa (with factor Va) after factor X is activated, not directly by factor VIIIa.

questions.stem: A medical student is counseling a family about the genetics of hemophilia A and B. Which of the following best describes the inheritance pattern of these two disorders?

explanation.summary: Both hemophilia A and B are X-linked recessive disorders.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both hemophilia A and hemophilia B are X-linked recessive disorders. Because males have only one X chromosome, they are affected far more often; females are usually protected by a second, normal X chromosome, though rare heterozygous females can be affected through unequal lyonization.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autosomal dominant is the pattern of von Willebrand disease, not hemophilia A or B.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autosomal recessive describes hemophilia C (factor XI), not hemophilia A or B.
whyWrongByChoice.D: X-linked dominant does not describe hemophilia; the disease is recessive and predominantly affects males.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitochondrial inheritance is unrelated to the factor VIII and IX genes involved in hemophilia.

questions.stem: A man with hemophilia B is planning to have children and is worried about passing on his bleeding disorder. His wife has no personal or family history of bleeding disorders. If the couple has a son, what is the chance the son will have hemophilia B?

explanation.summary: A son inherits his father's Y chromosome and his mother's X, so an affected father cannot pass X-linked hemophilia to a son.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemophilia B is X-linked recessive. A son receives his Y chromosome from his father and his single X chromosome from his unaffected mother, so he has no chance of inheriting the disease from his affected father. (In contrast, all daughters would receive the father's affected X and their mother's normal X, making them carriers.)

whyWrongByChoice.B: 25% would apply to certain autosomal recessive crosses, not to a son of an affected father in an X-linked disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50% would be the risk if the mother were a carrier, but she has no family history of bleeding disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 100% describes the carrier status of daughters, not the disease risk in sons.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The risk can be determined precisely from the X-linked recessive inheritance pattern; it is 0% for a son.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy with a suspected bleeding disorder undergoes coagulation testing. Which of the following combinations of results is most consistent with hemophilia?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Low platelet count with normal aPTT and PT

explanation.summary: Hemophilia impairs the intrinsic pathway, producing a prolonged aPTT with a normal PT.
explanation.whyCorrect: The aPTT measures the intrinsic coagulation pathway, while the PT measures the extrinsic pathway. Because hemophilia causes a defect in the intrinsic arm, the aPTT is prolonged. The PT remains normal because the extrinsic arm is unaffected.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A prolonged PT with a normal aPTT points to an extrinsic pathway defect, which is not the pattern in hemophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A prolonged PT and aPTT together suggest a common pathway or combined defect, not isolated hemophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Normal aPTT and PT would not explain the bleeding tendency of hemophilia, which prolongs the aPTT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemophilia does not lower the platelet count; a low platelet count suggests a platelet disorder instead.

questions.stem: A patient presents complaining of easy bruising and bleeding that takes a long time to stop. On examination, which of the following findings would suggest that the patient has a bleeding disorder OTHER than hemophilia?

explanation.summary: Petechiae reflect a platelet/vessel (primary hemostasis) problem and are characteristically absent in hemophilia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Disorders that impair platelet plug formation, such as von Willebrand disease, can be confused with hemophilia, but hemophilia patients lack petechiae. Petechiae result from bleeding caused by minor damage in capillary blood vessels, pointing to a primary hemostasis disorder rather than hemophilia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemarthrosis (bleeding into joints) is more common in hemophilia, so it supports rather than argues against the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A normal PT is a typical finding in hemophilia because the extrinsic pathway is unaffected.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A prolonged PTT is expected in hemophilia due to the intrinsic pathway defect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A family history of bleeding disorders is consistent with inherited hemophilia.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with mild hemophilia A due to reduced factor VIII production sustains a minor bleeding episode after a small laceration. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

explanation.summary: Desmopressin raises factor VIII levels and is appropriate for mild hemophilia A with reduced factor production.
explanation.whyCorrect: Desmopressin can be used to treat some patients with mild hemophilia A because it stimulates the release of von Willebrand factor from platelets, increasing factor VIII stability and leading to increased factor VIII levels. It is effective specifically in patients with reduced factor VIII production, as in this patient.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Recombinant factor IX is used to treat hemophilia B, not hemophilia A.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Recombinant factor VIIa is reserved for patients who have developed an inhibitor, not for a simple mild bleed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prothrombin complex concentrates are used to bypass an inhibitor, which this patient does not have.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fresh frozen plasma is considered contraindicated for hemophilia A because it contains only very low concentrations of factor VIII and is not extensively treated to prevent infection.

questions.stem: A boy with severe hemophilia A has been treated with recombinant factor VIII infusions for several years. He now has worsening bleeding, and repeat testing shows a prolonged PTT that fails to correct when mixed with normal plasma. Which of the following best explains this new finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of an antibody against factor VIII

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new deficiency of factor IX

explanation.summary: A prolonged PTT that no longer corrects with mixing indicates a factor VIII inhibitor.
explanation.whyCorrect: About 15% of patients with severe hemophilia A develop antibodies to recombinant factor VIII because their bodies recognize it as a foreign protein; this antibody is called an inhibitor. In these patients the prolonged PTT is not corrected when mixed with normal plasma because the antibodies bind the free factor VIII, preventing its function.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A new factor IX deficiency would still correct with a mixing study, since normal plasma supplies factor IX.
whyWrongByChoice.C: von Willebrand disease is inherited (autosomal dominant) and would not suddenly appear or cause failure of a mixing study in this way.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K deficiency mainly affects the PT and the vitamin K–dependent factors and corrects with mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombocytopenia is a low platelet count and would not cause a mixing study to fail to correct the PTT.

questions.stem: A patient with hemophilia A and a high-titer inhibitor develops an acute joint bleed. The team selects an agent that controls bleeding by directly generating factor Xa and thrombin, bypassing the need for factor VIII. Which of the following was most likely used?

explanation.summary: Recombinant factor VIIa bypasses an inhibitor by directly forming factor Xa and thrombin.
explanation.whyCorrect: For moderate- or high-titer inhibitors, patients can be given recombinant factor VIIa for acute bleeding because it directly forms factor Xa and thrombin, bypassing the inhibited factor VIII. This makes it well suited to control acute bleeding when a high-titer inhibitor is present.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Recombinant factor VIII would be bound and neutralized by the high-titer inhibitor, so it would not control bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Desmopressin only modestly raises factor VIII and is used for mild hemophilia A, not for a high-titer inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryoprecipitate is considered contraindicated in hemophilia A and does not bypass an inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Recombinant factor IX treats hemophilia B and does not bypass a factor VIII inhibitor.

questions.stem: A newborn boy with a family history of hemophilia A is found on factor assay to have less than 1% of normal factor VIII activity. Based on this factor level, which of the following bleeding patterns is he most likely to experience?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rare bleeding episodes, usually only after major trauma or surgery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bleeding only from mucosal surfaces with petechiae

choices.label: E
choices.text: Occasional trauma-induced bleeds that rarely cause crippling effects

explanation.summary: Factor activity below 1% defines severe hemophilia with frequent spontaneous bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the clinical classification of hemophilia, severe disease corresponds to less than 1% factor activity and is characterized by many bleeding episodes that are usually spontaneous; such patients rarely reach adulthood without treatment. This newborn's factor VIII activity below 1% places him in the severe category.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Rare bleeding only after major trauma or surgery describes mild disease (6–30% activity), not severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Even mild hemophilia carries a bleeding risk; a level below 1% produces frequent, often spontaneous bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mucosal bleeding with petechiae is characteristic of platelet/vWF disorders; hemophilia patients lack petechiae.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Occasional trauma-induced bleeds without crippling effects describes moderate disease (1–5% activity).

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis and no prior bleeding problems develops large spontaneous hematomas and muscle bleeds. Laboratory studies show a prolonged PTT that does not correct with a mixing study, and an antibody against factor VIII is identified. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A new factor VIII autoantibody in an older non-hemophiliac patient defines acquired hemophilia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acquired hemophilia arises when a non-hemophiliac patient develops an autoantibody against a specific coagulation factor, most commonly factor VIII. It tends to occur in older individuals with a female predominance and is associated with malignancy, autoimmune disease such as lupus, medications, or is idiopathic. Patients present with secondary hemostasis-type bleeding such as large hematomas and muscle bleeds, and the PTT does not correct with mixing because of the inhibitor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inherited hemophilia A presents in childhood and has a lifelong bleeding history, not new-onset bleeding at age 72.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemophilia C is a rare inherited factor XI deficiency, not an acquired factor VIII autoantibody.
whyWrongByChoice.D: von Willebrand disease is inherited and associated with petechiae and impaired platelet function, and its PTT would correct with mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin K deficiency prolongs the PT and corrects with a mixing study; it does not produce a factor VIII antibody.

questions.stem: A hematologist is explaining to trainees the most feared bleeding complication in patients with hemophilia. Which of the following is the most common cause of death from hemorrhage in these patients?

explanation.summary: Intracranial hemorrhage is the most common cause of death from hemorrhage in hemophilia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Additional sources of hemorrhage in hemophiliac patients include deep tissue bleeds and intracranial bleeds, and intracranial hemorrhage is identified as the most common cause of death from hemorrhage for patients with hemophilia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemarthrosis causes joint deformity and disability but is not the most common fatal hemorrhage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gastrointestinal bleeding causes blood in the stool but is not the leading cause of hemorrhagic death.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hematuria reflects genitourinary bleeding and is not typically fatal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cutaneous ecchymoses (easy bruising) is a common but superficial and non-fatal manifestation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with von Willebrand disease has severely reduced levels of von Willebrand factor (vWF). As a direct consequence of this deficiency, which of the following is most likely to undergo increased degradation?

explanation.summary: One of vWF's two major functions is to bind and stabilize circulating factor VIII.
explanation.whyCorrect: Factor VIII rapidly degrades unless it is bound to vWF, which acts as its protector in the circulation. When vWF is severely deficient, factor VIII is left unprotected and its levels fall; in a small number of cases the decrease is pronounced enough to mimic hemophilia A.

whyWrongByChoice.A: vWF anchors platelets to the subendothelium; its loss does not cause degradation of endothelial cells.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor VII is not bound or stabilized by vWF, so its degradation is unaffected.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factor IX does not interact with vWF; its deficiency causes hemophilia B, an unrelated disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelets are not degraded by loss of vWF; rather, they fail to adhere to the vessel wall.

questions.stem: A woman diagnosed with von Willebrand disease is found to be heterozygous for the gene defect. She is having a child with a man who has no personal or family history of a bleeding disorder. Assuming the disease is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion, what is the chance that their child will also have the disease?

explanation.summary: Most vWD is autosomal dominant, with affected people typically heterozygous for the defect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The father, having no signs of disease, is assumed homozygous for normal vWF, while the mother is heterozygous for the defective allele. In an autosomal dominant cross of a heterozygote with an unaffected homozygote, each child has a 50% chance of inheriting the defective allele and the disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A heterozygous affected parent does transmit the dominant allele to half of offspring, so risk is not zero.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 25% describes an autosomal recessive cross of two carriers, not this dominant scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 100% would require the affected parent to be homozygous, which is rare in dominant vWD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The inheritance pattern and parental genotypes are known, so the risk can be determined.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with von Willebrand disease has major bleeding, and recombinant vWF concentrate is not available at the facility. The team decides to give a blood product that provides a concentrated, bioactive source of von Willebrand factor. Which of the following treatments contains bioactive vWF?

explanation.summary: Cryoprecipitate is a concentrated source of clotting factors, including vWF, used when vWF concentrates are unavailable.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cryoprecipitate is a concentrated solution of clotting factors obtained by freezing and thawing fresh frozen plasma; it contains von Willebrand factor and serves as an alternative source when vWF concentrates are not available for major bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aminocaproic acid is an antifibrinolytic that prevents clot dissolution and contains no vWF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Desmopressin releases the patient's own stored vWF from endothelial cells but is not itself a source of vWF.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet transfusion supplies platelets to correct thrombocytopenia or aggregation defects, not vWF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic that prevents clot breakdown and contains no vWF.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old male is referred to the emergency department for refractory bleeding that began immediately after a wisdom tooth extraction and that the dentist was unable to stop. He reports lifelong weekly nosebleeds that take up to two hours to stop, and his father and sister have frequent nosebleeds. PT and PTT are normal. A CBC is normal, including a normal platelet count and normal platelet appearance on blood smear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Mucocutaneous bleeding with a positive family history and normal platelet count and coagulation times points to vWD.
explanation.whyCorrect: The picture of mucosal bleeding (nosebleeds, post-extraction bleeding), a family history suggesting autosomal dominant inheritance, and normal PT/PTT with a normal platelet count and smear is classic for von Willebrand disease. The diagnosis would be confirmed with vWF antigen and activity testing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bernard-Soulier syndrome, caused by a glycoprotein Ib mutation, presents with thrombocytopenia and abnormally large platelets, which are absent here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemophilia A is X-linked, typically affects males with deep tissue and joint bleeding and a prolonged PTT, not a normal PTT with only mucosal bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemophilia B is also X-linked with deep tissue and joint bleeding and a prolonged PTT, inconsistent with the normal PTT here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura involves platelet consumption with thrombocytopenia and thrombosis, not a normal platelet count with isolated mucosal bleeding.

questions.stem: When a blood vessel is injured, subendothelial collagen is exposed and von Willebrand factor acts as the glue that adheres platelets to this collagen. Binding of vWF to which of the following platelet receptors mediates this initial platelet adhesion?

explanation.summary: vWF adheres platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen through platelet glycoprotein Ib.
explanation.whyCorrect: In damaged vessels, vWF bridges platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen via platelet glycoprotein Ib (GpIb). This adhesion is the first step in building the platelet plug that prevents blood loss.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa binds fibrinogen to mediate platelet-to-platelet aggregation, not vWF-mediated adhesion to collagen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor VIII is a coagulation factor stabilized by vWF, not a platelet adhesion receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrinogen bridges platelets through GpIIb/IIIa during aggregation; it is not the receptor for vWF adhesion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The thrombin receptor participates in platelet activation but is not the site of vWF-mediated adhesion to subendothelium.

questions.stem: A newborn of consanguineous parents has severe bleeding involving both mucosal surfaces and a joint. Laboratory testing shows a virtually complete absence of von Willebrand factor along with markedly decreased factor VIII. Which type of von Willebrand disease does this child most likely have?

explanation.summary: Type 3 is the most severe form: complete absence of vWF, autosomal recessive, with decreased factor VIII.
explanation.whyCorrect: Type 3 vWD is the most severe form, characterized by complete absence of von Willebrand factor and inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion (fitting consanguinity). Because these patients also have decreased factor VIII, they experience both primary and secondary hemostasis bleeding, explaining the combined mucosal and joint bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type 1 is the mildest form with only a partial quantitative deficiency and intact function, not complete absence.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type 2 is a qualitative defect in which vWF is present but functionally abnormal, not absent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The recessive inheritance, consanguinity, and neonatal onset indicate an inherited absence rather than an acquired syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Type 2 subtypes are qualitative abnormalities beyond the brick's scope and do not feature complete absence of vWF.

questions.stem: During the workup of a patient with suspected von Willebrand disease, the laboratory adds the antibiotic ristocetin to the patient's blood in a test tube. In this assay, ristocetin is used to measure which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The function of von Willebrand factor

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total quantity of von Willebrand factor

choices.label: C
choices.text: The integrity of the intrinsic coagulation cascade

choices.label: D
choices.text: The amount of factor VIII in the blood

explanation.summary: The ristocetin cofactor assay measures vWF function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ristocetin activates von Willebrand factor so that it binds platelets, causing agglutination in the test tube. The degree of agglutination reflects how well the vWF functions, making this a test of vWF activity rather than quantity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The quantity of vWF is measured by a separate vWF antigen assay, not by ristocetin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The intrinsic coagulation cascade is assessed by the partial thromboplastin time, not the ristocetin assay.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factor VIII amount is measured by a factor VIII clotting activity assay, which is unrelated to ristocetin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibrinogen concentration is not measured by the ristocetin cofactor test.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is evaluated for easy bruising and heavy menses and is found to have a partial reduction in von Willebrand factor with normal vWF function; her family history suggests autosomal dominant inheritance. Which type of von Willebrand disease is she most likely to have, and how common is it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type 1, the mildest and most common form

explanation.summary: Type 1 is a partial quantitative deficiency with intact function, autosomal dominant, and the most common form.
explanation.whyCorrect: Type 1 vWD is the mildest and most common form, featuring a partial (quantitative) absence of vWF while function remains intact, and it is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion, matching this presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Type 2 is a qualitative abnormality (functionally abnormal vWF), not a partial quantitative deficiency with intact function, and is not described as the most common.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Type 3 is the most severe form with complete absence of vWF, not the most common or mildest.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type 2 is defined by qualitative dysfunction and is not the most severe form.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Type 3 is the most severe, not the mildest, form of vWD.

questions.stem: A brick describes a thrombotic disease that is, in a sense, the 'opposite' of von Willebrand disease. In this disorder there is impaired cleavage of ultra-large vWF multimers, leading initially to widespread platelet aggregation and thrombosis and eventually to depletion of circulating platelets. Which disorder is being described?

explanation.summary: TTP is the thrombotic 'opposite' of vWD, driven by impaired cleavage of ultra-large vWF multimers.
explanation.whyCorrect: In thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, ultra-large vWF multimers are not cleaved properly, producing excess vWF and widespread platelet aggregation (causing thrombosis), which eventually depletes circulating platelets and can then cause bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemophilia A is a factor VIII deficiency causing bleeding, not a thrombotic disorder from excess vWF multimers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Type 3 vWD is the complete absence of vWF causing severe bleeding, the opposite of excess vWF multimers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bernard-Soulier syndrome is a platelet receptor (GpIb) defect causing bleeding, not multimer-driven thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immune thrombocytopenia is platelet destruction by antibodies and does not involve impaired vWF multimer cleavage.

questions.stem: A patient with mild von Willebrand disease and residual endothelial stores of vWF has minor bleeding. The physician chooses desmopressin (DDAVP). By what mechanism does desmopressin help control bleeding in vWD?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It releases vWF from cellular storage compartments

choices.label: B
choices.text: It directly supplies recombinant vWF to the circulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: It prevents dissolution of already-formed clots

explanation.summary: Desmopressin releases the patient's own stored vWF from endothelial compartments.
explanation.whyCorrect: Desmopressin (DDAVP) works by releasing vWF from cellular compartments, making it useful in patients who have residual endothelial storage of vWF. Because not all patients have such stores, it does not work for everyone and is most often used in less severe bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Direct supplementation describes vWF replacement therapy, not desmopressin's mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Preventing clot dissolution is the mechanism of antifibrinolytics such as aminocaproic and tranexamic acid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Replacing platelets describes platelet transfusion, not desmopressin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cleaving ultra-large vWF multimers is the normal process impaired in TTP, unrelated to desmopressin.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with blood type O is found on routine testing to have a low vWF level that falls within the range seen in type 1 von Willebrand disease. He has no history of abnormal bleeding, easy bruising, or heavy bleeding after surgery, and no family history of a bleeding disorder. Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He should not be diagnosed with von Willebrand disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: He should be diagnosed with type 1 von Willebrand disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: He should be diagnosed with type 3 von Willebrand disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: He should immediately begin vWF replacement therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: He should be started on prophylactic desmopressin

explanation.summary: People with blood type O naturally have lower vWF; without bleeding symptoms they should not be diagnosed with vWD.
explanation.whyCorrect: People with blood type O generally have lower levels of vWF even without any vWD gene mutation. A person with type O and low vWF but no bleeding symptoms should not be diagnosed with von Willebrand disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A diagnosis of type 1 vWD is not appropriate when the low vWF is explained by blood type O and there are no bleeding symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Type 3 vWD is a severe disorder with complete absence of vWF and severe bleeding, not this asymptomatic mild reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: vWF replacement is reserved for bleeding or surgical prophylaxis in diagnosed disease, not an asymptomatic type O individual.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Desmopressin is a treatment for bleeding in diagnosed vWD, not indicated in an asymptomatic person who should not be diagnosed.

questions.stem: A patient has von Willebrand disease in which the measured quantity of von Willebrand factor is roughly normal, but the vWF that is present does not work properly. Based on this, how is this patient's disease best classified?

explanation.summary: Type 2 is a qualitative abnormality: vWF amount may be normal but it is functionally abnormal.
explanation.whyCorrect: Type 2 vWD is a qualitative abnormality in which the amount of vWF may be normal or decreased, but the vWF is functionally abnormal. In contrast, types 1 and 3 are quantitative abnormalities (partial or complete loss with normal function).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Type 1 is a quantitative abnormality with reduced amount but normal function, not a functional defect with normal quantity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Type 3 is a quantitative abnormality with complete absence of vWF, not normal quantity with abnormal function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type 1 is quantitative, not qualitative; this mislabels the abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Type 3 is quantitative (complete absence), not a qualitative abnormality.
