questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after collapsing at home. Over the past 2 days he had fever, a severe headache, and neck stiffness; he is now unresponsive. Temperature is 39.7°C (103.4°F), heart rate 126/min, and blood pressure 80/60 mm Hg. He has diffuse petechiae, and blood continues to ooze from the IV puncture site. Laboratory studies show a very low platelet count, prolonged PT and aPTT, and schistocytes on the peripheral smear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Sepsis with simultaneous bleeding and clotting, thrombocytopenia, schistocytes, and prolonged PT/aPTT is classic for DIC.
explanation.whyCorrect: DIC is a secondary thrombo-hemorrhagic disorder triggered by a severe underlying illness such as gram-negative sepsis. Rampant clotting consumes platelets and coagulation factors (causing prolonged PT and aPTT and bleeding), while fibrin strands shear red cells to produce schistocytes. The combination of petechiae, oozing lines, low platelets, prolonged coagulation times, and schistocytes in a septic patient is the signature of DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura causes isolated thrombocytopenia with normal PT/aPTT and no schistocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemophilia A is an X-linked factor VIII deficiency that prolongs the aPTT alone, with a normal platelet count and PT and no schistocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: von Willebrand disease produces mucocutaneous bleeding with a normal platelet count and normal PT, not this consumptive picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TTP also causes schistocytes and thrombocytopenia, but its coagulation studies (PT/aPTT) are characteristically normal, unlike the prolonged times seen here.

questions.stem: After a complicated, full-term delivery of her first child, a 23-year-old woman has a large postpartum hemorrhage that has been difficult to control over the past 24 hours. Which set of laboratory findings would be most indicative of disseminated intravascular coagulation in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Platelets increased, fibrinogen increased, PT decreased, aPTT decreased

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelets decreased, fibrinogen decreased, PT prolonged, aPTT prolonged

choices.label: C
choices.text: Platelets decreased, fibrinogen decreased, PT decreased, aPTT prolonged

choices.label: D
choices.text: Platelets increased, fibrinogen increased, PT prolonged, aPTT prolonged

choices.label: E
choices.text: Platelets decreased, fibrinogen increased, PT prolonged, aPTT decreased

explanation.summary: DIC consumes platelets and coagulation factors, giving low platelets, low fibrinogen, and prolonged PT and aPTT.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DIC, rampant clotting depletes platelets and clotting factors. This produces a decreased platelet count, decreased fibrinogen (fibrinogen is a coagulation factor converted into fibrin), and prolonged PT and aPTT. Obstetric complications are a classic trigger by dumping procoagulants into the blood.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased platelets and fibrinogen with shortened coagulation times is the opposite of the consumptive coagulopathy of DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A decreased (shortened) PT is inconsistent with DIC, where factor depletion prolongs the PT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelets and fibrinogen are consumed and therefore decreased in DIC, not increased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: In DIC fibrinogen is decreased and the aPTT is prolonged, not increased fibrinogen with a shortened aPTT.

questions.stem: A critically ill patient in the intensive care unit is suspected of having disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which of the following peripheral blood smear findings would most strongly support this diagnosis?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Large red blood cells with a purplish cast

choices.label: E
choices.text: Red blood cells with pointed contours and no central pallor

explanation.summary: Schistocytes, fragmented red cells with sharp angular contours, are the hallmark smear finding of the microangiopathy in DIC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Schistocytes are red cell fragments produced when red cells are sliced by sharp fibrin strands as they squeeze through microvascular clots. They are smaller than normal red cells and angular rather than round, lacking central pallor, and are never seen in normal blood, so their presence points to a microangiopathic process such as DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Blast cells suggest leukemia or a myeloproliferative disorder, not DIC itself.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Although platelets are decreased in DIC, thrombocytopenia has many more common causes and is nonspecific without schistocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An elevated white blood cell count reflects infection and is not itself indicative of DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Large red cells with a purplish cast are reticulocytes, seen in small numbers normally and increased in hemolysis generally, not specific for DIC.

questions.stem: In a patient with disseminated intravascular coagulation, how do fibrin degradation products directly contribute to the abnormal bleeding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They create new endothelial injury, exposing subendothelial collagen

explanation.summary: Fibrin degradation products are not inert; they actively inhibit new clot formation and worsen bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: As the body lyses the widespread clots of DIC, fibrin is broken down into fibrin degradation products. These fragments actively prevent the formation of new blood clots, aggravating the bleeding that is already occurring from consumption of platelets and coagulation factors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Creating new endothelial injury would promote clotting, not the anticoagulant, bleeding-promoting effect of FDPs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: FDPs do not act by inhibiting protein C; they directly interfere with clot formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Initiating widespread thrombosis would increase clotting, the opposite of how FDPs aggravate bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stimulating platelet aggregation would favor clotting rather than the bleeding caused by FDPs.

questions.stem: A patient is admitted with sepsis and develops disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to bacterial meningitis. Which of the following is the single most important treatment to manage the DIC?

explanation.summary: Because DIC is always secondary, the first and most important step is to treat the underlying condition, here with antibiotics.
explanation.whyCorrect: DIC is not a primary disorder; it is driven by an underlying severe illness. The first goal of management is to treat that trigger. For DIC secondary to bacterial meningitis and sepsis, antibiotics are the fundamental treatment. Blood-product transfusions are individualized and reserved for significant bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cryoprecipitate is used to replace fibrinogen and factor VIII when the patient is bleeding with low fibrinogen, not as the primary therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fresh frozen plasma corrects a prolonged PT and aPTT in a bleeding patient but does not address the underlying infection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heparin is reserved for thrombosis-predominant DIC with large thrombi or tissue necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet transfusion is used for significant bleeding with thrombocytopenia, not to cure the underlying cause.

questions.stem: A patient has schistocytes and thrombocytopenia on evaluation, raising concern for a thrombotic microangiopathy. Which laboratory feature best distinguishes disseminated intravascular coagulation from the other thrombotic microangiopathies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of schistocytes on the smear

explanation.summary: Prolongation of the coagulation times (PT and aPTT) sets DIC apart from other thrombotic microangiopathies.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DIC, rampant fibrin production depletes coagulation factors, so the PT, INR, aPTT, and thrombin time are all prolonged. This distinguishes DIC from other forms of thrombotic microangiopathy, in which the coagulation times are typically normal because factors are not consumed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Schistocytes appear in any thrombotic microangiopathy, so they do not distinguish DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A low platelet count is common to all thrombotic microangiopathies and is nonspecific.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Elevated indirect bilirubin reflects hemolysis, which occurs in any microangiopathic process, not just DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D-dimer is elevated whenever clots break down and is sensitive but nonspecific, so it does not separate DIC from other microangiopathies.

questions.stem: A patient with gram-negative sepsis develops disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which of the following best describes the mechanism that inappropriately activates the coagulation cascade in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vessel wall injury releases tumor necrosis factor, prompting endothelial cells to release tissue factor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Circulating antibodies destroy platelets in the spleen

choices.label: C
choices.text: A deficiency of ADAMTS13 allows large von Willebrand multimers to accumulate

choices.label: E
choices.text: A congenital deficiency of protein C removes an anticoagulant brake

explanation.summary: Endothelial injury releases TNF, which drives endothelial cells to release tissue factor and activate the extrinsic pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DIC, vessel wall injury releases tumor necrosis factor, which causes endothelial cells to release tissue factor. Tissue factor is the stimulus that activates the extrinsic coagulation pathway, and the cascade becomes supercharged, generating excessive fibrin in vessels throughout the body.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Splenic antibody-mediated platelet destruction describes immune thrombocytopenia, not the tissue-factor mechanism of DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ADAMTS13 deficiency with large von Willebrand multimers is the mechanism of TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Autoantibodies against factor VIII describe acquired hemophilia, not DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Congenital protein C deficiency is an inherited thrombophilia, not the acquired tissue-factor-driven activation seen in DIC.

questions.stem: A patient with disseminated intravascular coagulation is found to be anemic, and the smear shows numerous fragmented red cells. Which of the following best explains the origin of these red cell fragments?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antibody-mediated destruction of red cells by the spleen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red cells being sliced by fibrin strands

choices.label: C
choices.text: Osmotic swelling and rupture of red cells in hypotonic plasma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxidative denaturation of hemoglobin forming Heinz bodies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Impaired erythropoietin production reducing red cell survival

explanation.summary: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia in DIC results from red cells being sheared by intravascular fibrin strands.
explanation.whyCorrect: Red cells are pliable but cannot easily flex around the sharp fibrin strands of forming microvascular clots. Some are destroyed completely, and others are sliced into fragments that reseal as schistocytes. This mechanical shearing produces microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antibody-mediated splenic destruction describes autoimmune hemolytic anemia, which produces spherocytes, not shear fragments.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Osmotic rupture in hypotonic plasma is not the mechanism; hemolysis in DIC is mechanical.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heinz bodies from oxidative injury are characteristic of G6PD deficiency, not microangiopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reduced erythropoietin lowers production but does not fragment circulating red cells.

questions.stem: A patient with disseminated intravascular coagulation is actively bleeding, and laboratory studies show a markedly low fibrinogen level. Which of the following blood-derived products is most appropriate to replace the fibrinogen?

explanation.summary: Cryoprecipitate replaces fibrinogen (and factor VIII) in a bleeding DIC patient with a low fibrinogen level.
explanation.whyCorrect: When bleeding in DIC is being corrected with transfusions, cryoprecipitate is given specifically to replace fibrinogen and factor VIII. It is the product of choice for correcting the hypofibrinogenemia caused by consumption of fibrinogen during widespread clotting.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Packed red blood cells restore oxygen-carrying capacity in anemia but do not replace fibrinogen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Platelet concentrate replaces consumed platelets, not fibrinogen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intravenous immunoglobulin has no role in replacing clotting factors in DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heparin is an anticoagulant used for thrombosis-predominant DIC and would worsen bleeding, not replace fibrinogen.

questions.stem: A patient with disseminated intravascular coagulation develops progressive ischemia and early gangrene of several fingers and toes from large occlusive thrombi. Bleeding is minimal. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this thrombosis-predominant presentation?

explanation.summary: When thrombosis predominates with large thrombi or tissue necrosis, anticoagulation with heparin is used.
explanation.whyCorrect: If thrombosis is the predominant feature of DIC, treatment must weigh the risk of bleeding against the risk of further clot formation. For large thrombi or tissue necrosis, such as digital gangrene, anticoagulation with heparin is used to limit ongoing clotting.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fresh frozen plasma replaces coagulation factors for bleeding and would not address the thrombotic complication.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Platelet transfusion is for bleeding with thrombocytopenia and could add fuel to thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryoprecipitate replaces fibrinogen for bleeding, not thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Packed red blood cells treat anemia and have no effect on the occlusive thrombi.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with newly diagnosed pancreatic adenocarcinoma is found to have laboratory evidence of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which of the following best describes how this malignancy triggers DIC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It releases procoagulant substances into the circulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: It directly damages the vascular endothelium with bacterial toxins

choices.label: D
choices.text: It causes an inherited deficiency of antithrombin

choices.label: E
choices.text: It inflames blood vessel walls as a vasculitis

explanation.summary: Mucinous adenocarcinomas trigger DIC as 'dumpers' by releasing procoagulant substances into the blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: The causes of DIC can be divided into dumpers (things that dump procoagulants into the blood) and rippers (things that damage the endothelium). Adenocarcinomas, especially very mucinous ones like pancreatic adenocarcinoma, are classic dumpers that release procoagulant material into the circulation and set off the coagulation cascade.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Toxin-mediated endothelial damage describes a ripper such as gram-negative sepsis, not a mucinous tumor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antiplatelet autoantibodies describe immune thrombocytopenia, not the mechanism of malignancy-associated DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inherited antithrombin deficiency is a congenital thrombophilia, not how adenocarcinoma triggers DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vasculitis (e.g., in systemic lupus erythematosus) is a ripper that damages endothelium, not the procoagulant-dumping mechanism of adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: A patient with disseminated intravascular coagulation and a very low platelet count is examined. Which of the following is the most common skin manifestation of DIC?

explanation.summary: Petechiae are the most common skin manifestation of DIC, reflecting microvascular hemorrhage from thrombocytopenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The skin in DIC commonly shows petechiae, the pinpoint microvascular hemorrhages typical of any patient with a low platelet count. Because DIC consumes platelets, petechiae are the most common cutaneous finding, and patients may also bleed from intravenous and other line sites.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A malar rash is a feature of systemic lupus erythematosus, not a manifestation of DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Palpable purpura from immune-complex deposition characterizes a primary small-vessel vasculitis, not the platelet-poor microhemorrhages of DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythema nodosum is a panniculitis associated with infections and sarcoidosis, unrelated to DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Urticaria is an allergic or histamine-mediated reaction, not a consequence of consumptive thrombocytopenia.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is diagnosed with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome after presenting with a deep vein thrombosis and two prior miscarriages. She has no personal or family history of a hereditary clotting disorder. Which of the following underlying conditions is she most likely to also have?

explanation.summary: Systemic lupus erythematosus is the systemic disease most commonly associated with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: APS is associated with other autoimmune diseases, most commonly systemic lupus erythematosus, which is present about 50% of the time. Roughly 20% to 40% of patients with SLE demonstrate antiphospholipid antibodies, and this is the most common cause of secondary APS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factor V Leiden is a hereditary thrombotic disorder, not an autoimmune disease linked to APS.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Multiple myeloma is a neoplastic proliferation of plasma cells producing monoclonal antibodies and is not associated with APS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Protein C deficiency is another inherited thrombophilia, unrelated to the autoimmune basis of APS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemophilia A is an inherited bleeding disorder, the opposite of the prothrombotic state seen in APS.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old woman with a history of deep vein thrombosis and two spontaneous miscarriages, who is otherwise healthy, presents to the clinic for evaluation. Which of the following laboratory findings would be most consistent with a diagnosis of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome?

explanation.summary: APS characteristically produces a paradoxically prolonged aPTT despite the hypercoagulable in vivo state.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antiphospholipid antibodies bind and inhibit the thromboplastin reagent used in coagulation studies, prolonging the aPTT in vitro even though the antibodies act as procoagulants in vivo. A prolonged aPTT is therefore a supportive laboratory finding in APS.

whyWrongByChoice.B: APS is not associated with an elevated INR; the characteristic in vitro abnormality is a prolonged aPTT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leukocytosis is not a feature of APS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The aPTT is characteristically prolonged in APS, not normal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: APS is associated with thrombocytopenia from platelet consumption in clots, not thrombocytosis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome is examined in the clinic. Which of the following skin findings is most likely to be present?

explanation.summary: Livedo reticularis, a mottled net-like skin discoloration, is the characteristic cutaneous finding in APS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Livedo reticularis is a clinical finding of APS caused by obstruction of superficial capillaries by small blood clots. It reflects the prothrombotic nature of the disease at the level of the skin microvasculature.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epistaxis is associated with bleeding disorders such as clotting factor deficiencies, not the prothrombotic state of APS.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gum bleeding likewise reflects a bleeding tendency rather than the thrombosis seen in APS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Palmar erythema is associated with liver disease, not APS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spider angiomas are also associated with liver disease rather than APS.

questions.stem: A medical student is reviewing the pathophysiology of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. Which of the following statements is most accurate about the "two-hit" theory of APS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antiphospholipid antibodies are the only component of APS pathophysiology.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Risk factors such as smoking and surgery prevent the full manifestation of APS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The presence of antiphospholipid antibodies alone does not lead to the full manifestation of APS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Two incidents of blunt-force trauma can lead to the development of APS.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Two or more thrombosis risk factors are required to diagnose APS.

explanation.summary: The two-hit theory holds that antiphospholipid antibodies plus an additional hypercoagulable insult are needed for clinical APS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because antiphospholipid antibodies alone do not lead to APS, researchers proposed a two-hit theory: the first hit is the presence of the antibodies, and the second hit is an additional risk factor for hypercoagulability. The antibodies alone do not produce the full manifestation of disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The theory specifically states the antibodies are not the only component; a second hit is required.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factors such as smoking and surgery deliver the second hit that unmasks APS; they do not prevent it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The two hits refer to antibodies plus a hypercoagulable risk factor, not blunt-force trauma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The theory does not specify a required number of additional conventional risk factors for the full development of APS.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old man with a recent diagnosis of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome presents to the hospital with a deep venous thrombosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for his condition?

explanation.summary: APS-associated thrombosis is treated with anticoagulation, then long-term warfarin therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with APS and thrombi are treated with anticoagulants, just like any patient with a thrombus, and are kept on long-term anticoagulation because of the risk of re-thrombosis. Most physicians use warfarin for ongoing prophylaxis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Desmopressin is used for bleeding disorders such as von Willebrand disease and hemophilia, not for thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor replacement is used for bleeding disorders from factor deficiency, the opposite of the APS prothrombotic state.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet transfusion treats disorders of platelet number or function causing bleeding, not APS thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fresh frozen plasma replaces clotting factors for bleeding and would not treat an APS thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with an unexplained deep vein thrombosis has a positive lupus anticoagulant on initial testing. Her physician wants to confirm a diagnosis of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step regarding laboratory confirmation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Repeat the antibody testing at least 12 weeks later

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diagnose APS immediately based on the single positive result

choices.label: C
choices.text: Repeat the antibody testing after 48 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Order genetic testing for Factor V Leiden

explanation.summary: APS lab abnormalities must be confirmed on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart.
explanation.whyCorrect: A positive antiphospholipid antibody must be confirmed on 2 occasions at least 12 weeks apart. This interval excludes transient, insignificant antibodies, such as those following a viral infection, which represent the majority of positive results and would not indicate true APS.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A single positive test may reflect a transient antibody; diagnosis requires confirmation on two occasions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Retesting after 48 hours is far too soon; the confirmatory interval is at least 12 weeks.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A bone marrow biopsy is not part of the diagnostic workup for APS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factor V Leiden is a separate hereditary thrombophilia and does not confirm APS.

questions.stem: A patient with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome is noted to have a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time, even though the patient is clinically in a hypercoagulable state with recurrent thrombosis. Which of the following best explains this paradoxical laboratory result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The antibodies bind and inhibit the thromboplastin reagent

choices.label: B
choices.text: The antibodies deplete circulating fibrinogen in the patient

choices.label: C
choices.text: The antibodies cause a true deficiency of factor VIII

choices.label: D
choices.text: The antibodies destroy platelets, prolonging the clotting time

choices.label: E
choices.text: The antibodies activate the fibrinolytic system in vivo

explanation.summary: Antiphospholipid antibodies inhibit the thromboplastin reagent in vitro, prolonging the aPTT even though they are procoagulant in vivo.
explanation.whyCorrect: The prolonged aPTT occurs because antiphospholipid antibodies bind to and inhibit the reagent (thromboplastin) used in the coagulation studies. This means the antibodies inhibit coagulation in vitro, whereas they act as procoagulants in vivo, producing the apparent paradox.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The prolonged aPTT reflects interference with the assay reagent, not fibrinogen depletion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no true factor VIII deficiency; the antibodies interfere with the test, not with factor synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although thrombocytopenia can occur in APS, platelet destruction does not explain the prolonged aPTT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The abnormality is an in vitro artifact of reagent inhibition, not fibrinolytic activation in vivo.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome and prior pregnancy loss becomes pregnant again and is referred to a high-risk pregnancy specialist. Which of the following regimens is most appropriate to reduce her risk of recurrent pregnancy loss?

explanation.summary: Pregnant APS patients are managed with low-molecular-weight heparin and low-dose aspirin.
explanation.whyCorrect: For women with APS who become pregnant, anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin and low-dose aspirin is often recommended to try to avoid recurrent loss of the pregnancy. This differs from the long-term warfarin used outside of pregnancy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pregnant APS patients are not usually treated with long-term warfarin; warfarin is used for non-pregnant thrombosis prophylaxis and is generally avoided in pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Corticosteroids alone are not the recommended regimen for preventing recurrent APS-related pregnancy loss.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Desmopressin is used for bleeding disorders and has no role in preventing APS pregnancy loss.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Treatment is recommended; low-molecular-weight heparin and low-dose aspirin reduce the risk of recurrent loss.

questions.stem: A physician is deciding which laboratory studies to order to confirm antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. Which of the following antibodies is one of the accepted criteria for the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I is one of the three antibodies whose presence supports a diagnosis of APS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Labs indicating APS include the presence in serum of lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin antibodies, or anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I antibodies. Anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I is one of these three confirmatory criterion antibodies.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anti-double-stranded DNA is specific for SLE, not a diagnostic criterion for APS itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody is associated with rheumatoid arthritis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anti-smooth muscle antibody is associated with autoimmune hepatitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anti-mitochondrial antibody is associated with primary biliary cholangitis.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is found to have circulating antiphospholipid antibodies but has never had a thrombosis or pregnancy loss. According to the two-hit theory, which of the following additional factors would most likely serve as a second hit that could precipitate the full manifestation of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome?

explanation.summary: A second hit is an additional hypercoagulable risk factor such as oral contraceptive use.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the two-hit theory, the first hit is the presence of antiphospholipid antibodies and the second hit is any additional risk factor for hypercoagulability, such as smoking, surgery, diabetes, pregnancy, oral contraceptive intake, or malignancy. Starting an oral contraceptive is an example of such a second hit.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A low-sodium diet is not a recognized hypercoagulable risk factor for the APS second hit.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A daily multivitamin does not promote hypercoagulability and is not a second hit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aerobic exercise is not among the listed prothrombotic second-hit risk factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reducing caffeine intake does not increase clotting risk and is not a second hit.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man presents with a myocardial infarction and has no family history of heart disease and no traditional cardiac risk factors. Workup reveals antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. Which of the following best describes the epidemiologic significance of this disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the most common acquired disorder of thrombosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the most common inherited disorder of thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is primarily a disorder of impaired platelet production

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is most common in men over the age of 60

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is a benign condition that never causes organ damage

explanation.summary: APS is the most common acquired disorder of thrombosis and can strike young patients without other risk factors.
explanation.whyCorrect: APS is an autoimmune disorder characterized by prothrombotic circulating autoantibodies and is the most common acquired disorder of thrombosis. Some patients present in their 20s with myocardial infarction or stroke in the absence of any family history of heart disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: APS is acquired and autoimmune, not an inherited thrombophilia like Factor V Leiden.
whyWrongByChoice.C: APS is a prothrombotic disorder; any thrombocytopenia results from platelet consumption, not impaired production.
whyWrongByChoice.D: APS often affects young adults, including women of reproductive age, rather than being a disorder of older men.
whyWrongByChoice.E: APS can cause serious thrombotic complications including DVT, PE, stroke, myocardial infarction, and fetal loss.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman has had three spontaneous abortions before 10 weeks of gestation with no identifiable cause. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her evaluation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reassure her that testing is not warranted

choices.label: D
choices.text: Order a peripheral blood smear only

explanation.summary: Recurrent unexplained pregnancy loss is an indication to test for antiphospholipid antibody syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Testing for APS is indicated in patients with one or more unexplained episodes of pregnancy loss beyond 10 weeks, or three or more spontaneous abortions before 10 weeks. This patient's history of three early losses meets that threshold and warrants antiphospholipid antibody testing.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Her history of three unexplained early losses is exactly the scenario in which APS testing is recommended.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Empiric warfarin before establishing a diagnosis is inappropriate and is not the management of APS in pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A peripheral smear alone would not detect antiphospholipid antibodies or establish the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Splenectomy has no role in the evaluation of recurrent pregnancy loss due to suspected APS.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman is evaluated for a second unprovoked deep vein thrombosis. Genetic testing reveals a factor V Leiden mutation. Which of the following best explains why this mutation predisposes her to thrombosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mutant factor V is resistant to inactivation by activated protein C

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mutant factor V cannot interact with factor X

choices.label: C
choices.text: The mutant factor V is immediately cleaved to its inactive form

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mutant factor V is present at markedly reduced levels

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mutant factor V shows increased reactivity to prothrombin

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden is an abnormal factor V that cannot be cleaved and inactivated by protein C, so clotting is not turned off normally.
explanation.whyCorrect: The factor V Leiden gene carries a single-point mutation causing an arginine-to-glutamine substitution at the exact site where protein C normally cleaves and inactivates factor V. The abnormal factor V participates in coagulation like normal factor V, but when it is time to stop making fibrin, protein C cannot inactivate it, so thrombosis increases abnormally.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor V Leiden interacts with the cascade just like normal factor V; its interaction with other factors is preserved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The opposite is true: the whole problem is that factor V Leiden cannot be cleaved to its inactive form.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factor V Leiden is present at normal levels; the defect is functional resistance to protein C, not deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factor V Leiden shows no change in reactivity to prothrombin.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old man presents to the emergency department with his third episode of unilateral lower-extremity swelling and pain. He is diagnosed with a deep vein thrombosis, and thrombophilia workup is positive for a mutation in the gene encoding factor II. Which of the following best explains how this mutation leads to hypercoagulability?

explanation.summary: The prothrombin G20210A mutation causes overproduction of factor II, providing more substrate for thrombin generation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The factor II (prothrombin G20210A) mutation is a point mutation substituting guanine for adenine at position 20210 of the prothrombin gene, leading to overproduction of factor II. More prothrombin means more thrombin, which converts more fibrinogen to fibrin and increases the tendency to clot.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased antithrombin describes antithrombin deficiency, a separate disorder, not the factor II mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The mutation raises the quantity of prothrombin produced, not the efficiency of activating existing prothrombin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombin activity itself is not intrinsically altered; there is simply more of it because there is more prothrombin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Resistance of clotting factors to antithrombin is not the mechanism of the factor II mutation.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with several prior episodes of deep vein thrombosis is found to have a deficiency in protein C. Which of the following mechanisms most directly accounts for her recurrent thrombosis?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased activity of factors Va and VIIIa

explanation.summary: Protein C normally inactivates factors Va and VIIIa; with deficiency, these factors remain active and clotting continues unchecked.
explanation.whyCorrect: Activated protein C terminates coagulation by directly inactivating factors Va and VIIIa. When protein C is deficient, factors Va and VIIIa retain increased activity, so the coagulation cascade is not turned off and thrombosis increases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A factor Va that is resistant to deactivation describes factor V Leiden; protein C deficiency instead leaves normal Va and VIIIa unopposed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased blood stasis is an acquired, provoked cause of DVT (e.g., prolonged immobility), not the mechanism of protein C deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased prothrombin production is the mechanism of the factor II gene mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Loss of cofactor activity describes protein S deficiency (protein S is the cofactor for protein C).

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department with left lower-leg swelling and pain and reports multiple prior similar episodes. He has no established risk factors for thrombosis. Which of the following underlying disorders is most likely responsible for his recurrent symptoms?

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden is the most common hereditary thrombotic disorder and is the most likely cause of unexplained recurrent DVT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent unprovoked venous thrombi in a young patient without classic risk factors suggest an inherited thrombophilia. Factor V Leiden is the most common hereditary thrombotic disorder, accounting for 40-50% of unexplained thrombosis, making it the single best answer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antiphospholipid syndrome can cause DVT but is an acquired disorder classically associated with recurrent miscarriage/stillbirth and is less likely than factor V Leiden here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Malignancy causes a hypercoagulable state, but nothing suggests cancer in this young, otherwise healthy patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Protein C deficiency causes thrombosis but is considerably less common than factor V Leiden.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The prothrombin gene mutation also causes hypercoagulability but is less common than factor V Leiden.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with sudden redness and swelling of his right leg; compression Doppler ultrasound confirms a femoral vein DVT, and testing shows he is heterozygous for the factor V Leiden mutation. His family history includes a father with a pulmonary embolism and a sister with a pregnancy-associated clot. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for his DVT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin, elevation, and warm soaks to the leg

explanation.summary: A femoral DVT is treated with anticoagulation; heparin bridges to warfarin, and the inherited thrombophilia does not change this initial treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Standard treatment uses low-molecular-weight heparin together with warfarin, with heparin acting as a bridge until warfarin becomes therapeutic (about 2 days). The presence of an inherited thrombophilia such as factor V Leiden does not change the initial treatment of the DVT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin, elevation, and warm soaks are not adequate therapy for a femoral vein DVT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Heparin alone is incomplete because heparin is parenteral; since months of anticoagulation are needed, an oral agent such as warfarin is preferred for long-term therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Warfarin alone is inappropriate initially because it takes several days to become therapeutic and starting it without a bridge can transiently worsen thrombosis or cause skin necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An IVC filter is not first-line treatment for an uncomplicated DVT that can be anticoagulated.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with protein C deficiency is started on warfarin for a deep vein thrombosis and, days later, develops painful erythematous skin lesions that progress to necrosis. Which of the following best explains this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin lowers protein C first, causing a transient hypercoagulable state

choices.label: B
choices.text: Warfarin directly activates factors Va and VIIIa

choices.label: C
choices.text: Warfarin triggers an immune-mediated destruction of platelets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warfarin causes immediate depletion of fibrinogen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Warfarin increases antithrombin binding to thrombin

explanation.summary: Warfarin-induced skin necrosis reflects an early, transient hypercoagulable state because protein C (short half-life) falls before the procoagulant factors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Warfarin blocks vitamin K-dependent factors, which include proteins C and S as well as factors II, VII, IX, and X. Protein C and factor VII have the shortest half-lives, so protein C activity falls first; in a patient with baseline protein C deficiency this produces a transient hypercoagulable state that causes skin and subcutaneous thrombosis and necrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Warfarin does not activate clotting factors; it blocks synthesis of vitamin K-dependent factors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune-mediated platelet destruction describes heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, not warfarin-induced skin necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Warfarin does not directly deplete fibrinogen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increasing antithrombin binding to thrombin is the mechanism of heparin, not warfarin.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a known thrombophilia is admitted with a new DVT and started on standard weight-based heparin, but she fails to achieve a therapeutic level despite escalating doses. Which of the following inherited disorders best explains her heparin resistance?

explanation.summary: Heparin acts through antithrombin, so antithrombin deficiency reduces heparin's effect and produces heparin resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heparin works by binding antithrombin and inducing a conformational change that increases antithrombin's affinity for thrombin and factor X, dramatically increasing its inactivating ability. In antithrombin deficiency, reduced antithrombin activity leads to a reduced effect of heparin, so patients often need higher doses, antithrombin supplementation, or an agent not dependent on antithrombin such as dabigatran.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factor V Leiden causes protein C resistance, not heparin resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The prothrombin mutation increases prothrombin production and does not impair heparin's action.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Protein C deficiency is associated with warfarin-induced skin necrosis, not heparin resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Protein S deficiency impairs protein C function but does not cause heparin resistance.

questions.stem: A researcher is describing the normal regulation of coagulation. Antithrombin limits clot formation primarily by which of the following actions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibiting thrombin and other activated factors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Directly cleaving and inactivating factors Va and VIIIa

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serving as a cofactor that enables protein C

explanation.summary: Antithrombin is an enzyme inhibitor that inhibits thrombin and multiple activated coagulation factors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antithrombin inhibits coagulation by inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) as well as activated factors VIIa, IXa, Xa, XIa, and XIIa. Individuals with antithrombin deficiency lose this braking action and are prone to thrombotic episodes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Directly inactivating factors Va and VIIIa is the role of activated protein C, not antithrombin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acting as a cofactor for protein C describes protein S.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reducing synthesis of prothrombin is not how antithrombin works; warfarin blocks synthesis of vitamin K-dependent factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Converting fibrinogen to fibrin is the procoagulant action of thrombin, the opposite of antithrombin's role.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with recurrent unprovoked venous thrombosis is undergoing a thrombophilia workup, and antithrombin deficiency is suspected. Which of the following is the most appropriate test to confirm this diagnosis?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Genetic testing for the factor V Leiden mutation

explanation.summary: Antithrombin deficiency is confirmed with a functional plasma antithrombin activity (AT-heparin cofactor) assay.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because hereditary antithrombin deficiency is not defined by a single specific mutation, a functional test is used: measuring plasma antithrombin activity with the AT-heparin cofactor assay. This detects both low levels and defective antithrombin function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The activated protein C resistance assay is used to detect factor V Leiden, not antithrombin deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Factor II gene analysis detects the prothrombin G20210A mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A serum protein S level evaluates protein S deficiency, a different disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factor V Leiden genetic testing evaluates factor V Leiden, not antithrombin deficiency.

questions.stem: A neonate with a severe inherited deficiency of protein C develops sudden-onset widespread hemorrhagic necrosis and infarction of the skin along with laboratory evidence of consumptive coagulopathy. Which of the following conditions best describes this presentation?

explanation.summary: Severe protein C or S deficiency can cause purpura fulminans, with widespread microthrombi producing hemorrhagic skin necrosis and frequently DIC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with protein C or S deficiency can present with purpura fulminans, an acute disorder of nonstop thrombotic complications. They develop sudden-onset hemorrhagic necrosis and infarction of the skin from widespread multiple thrombi and frequently have associated disseminated intravascular coagulation, often with fatal consequences.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Warfarin-induced skin necrosis is triggered by starting warfarin, whereas this neonate developed spontaneous necrosis from severe inherited deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura causes bleeding from low platelets, not widespread thrombotic skin infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A DVT is a localized deep venous clot, not diffuse hemorrhagic skin necrosis with DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary embolism presents with dyspnea and chest pain, not widespread cutaneous necrosis.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a genetic thrombophilia and a prior deep vein thrombosis becomes pregnant and asks how her clotting risk will be managed. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach for a high-risk patient like her?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give anticoagulants both before and after delivery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin lifelong high-intensity anticoagulation for all carriers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Withhold all anticoagulation because most carriers never clot

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use daily aspirin as the sole agent throughout pregnancy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Start warfarin at high doses during the first trimester

explanation.summary: Pregnant patients with a history of thrombi and other high-risk features are given anticoagulants before and after delivery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prophylaxis decisions are individualized, but pregnant patients with a history of thrombi and some high-risk patients are given anticoagulants both before and after delivery, because pregnancy substantially raises thrombotic risk.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Most patients with genetic thrombophilia never develop thrombosis, so lifelong high-intensity anticoagulation for all carriers is not warranted.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Although most carriers never clot, this patient is high risk with a prior thrombus and does warrant prophylaxis during pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin alone is not the standard prophylaxis for a high-risk pregnant patient with prior venous thrombi.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin is generally avoided in pregnancy, and high-dose warfarin in the first trimester would be inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman is evaluated for abdominal pain and ascites and is found to have a thrombus in her hepatic vein. She has had two prior spontaneous miscarriages, and her mother had a pulmonary embolism at age 40. Which of the following features most strongly suggests an underlying thrombotic disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: She is currently taking a combined oral contraceptive

choices.label: B
choices.text: She recently returned from a long-haul flight

choices.label: C
choices.text: The thrombus is located in the hepatic vein

choices.label: D
choices.text: She has a body mass index of 31

choices.label: E
choices.text: She smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily

explanation.summary: A thrombus in an unusual site (not the legs or lungs) is a red flag for an inherited thrombotic disorder.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists a venous thrombus somewhere other than the legs or lungs among the signs that should raise suspicion for an underlying thrombotic disorder. A hepatic vein thrombus is an unusual location and, together with her young age, recurrent family thromboses, and history of miscarriage, points toward a hereditary disorder rather than an ordinary provoked clot.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral contraceptive use is a well-known acquired risk factor for thrombi and would not by itself point to an underlying disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Immobility from prolonged travel is a common provoking factor and does not suggest a hereditary disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Obesity increases thrombotic risk in the general population and is not a clue to an inherited disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Smoking is a common acquired risk factor that raises clot likelihood without indicating an underlying disorder.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with recurrent deep vein thromboses undergoes coagulation testing. Laboratory studies show a factor V protein that participates in the clotting cascade but cannot be turned off normally. Which of the following best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A point mutation makes factor V resistant to inactivation by protein C

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess prothrombin drives overproduction of thrombin

choices.label: C
choices.text: A deficiency of antithrombin removes a broad inhibitor of the cascade

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antibodies to phospholipids damage the vascular endothelium

choices.label: E
choices.text: A deficiency of protein S impairs inactivation of factor VIIIa

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden results from a point mutation that renders factor V resistant to inactivation by protein C.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that factor V Leiden is caused by a point mutation in the factor V gene that makes the resulting factor V resistant to inactivation by protein C. The factor continues to participate in the cascade but cannot be turned off, so fibrin production continues inappropriately.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Excess prothrombin describes the factor II gene mutation, which raises thrombin and fibrin rather than making factor V resistant to inactivation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antithrombin deficiency removes a general inhibitor of the cascade; it does not alter factor V's susceptibility to protein C.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Antiphospholipid antibodies are an acquired mechanism acting on endothelium and platelets, not a mutation in factor V.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Protein S deficiency impairs the inhibitors, but the defect here is in factor V itself, not in a missing inhibitor.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common of all the thrombotic disorders?

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden is the most common thrombotic disorder.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explicitly identifies factor V Leiden as the most common of all the thrombotic disorders, resulting from a point mutation that makes factor V resistant to inactivation by protein C.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antithrombin deficiency is described as a rare genetic defect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Protein C deficiency is one of the less common inherited disorders and is not the most common overall.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The factor II (prothrombin) gene mutation is described as a rare disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome is the main acquired disorder but is not the most common thrombotic disorder overall.

questions.stem: Which of the following thrombotic disorders is acquired rather than hereditary?

explanation.summary: Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome is the main acquired thrombotic disorder; the others are hereditary.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick presents antiphospholipid antibody syndrome as the main acquired thrombotic disorder, in which patients make antibodies to phospholipids that damage endothelium and platelets, leading to excessive clotting. It can be primary or part of a systemic disease such as lupus.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Antithrombin deficiency is a hereditary genetic defect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor V Leiden is a hereditary disorder caused by a point mutation in the factor V gene.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Protein C deficiency is a hereditary abnormality of a clotting inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Protein S deficiency is a hereditary abnormality of a clotting inhibitor.

questions.stem: A patient is started on warfarin for a thrombotic disorder. By inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase, warfarin reduces the synthesis of which of the following groups of factors?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Factors II, VII, IX, and X plus proteins C and S

explanation.summary: Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist that lowers the vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C and S.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that warfarin interferes with the gamma-carboxylation of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors by inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase. The specific vitamin K-dependent factors are proteins C and S and factors II, VII, IX, and X.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factors V and VIII are cofactors that are not vitamin K-dependent and are not directly reduced by warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factors XI and XII are not among the vitamin K-dependent factors affected by warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrinogen (factor I) and factor XIII act in the final steps of clot formation and are not vitamin K-dependent.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antithrombin and plasminogen are not vitamin K-dependent clotting factors targeted by warfarin.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known hereditary protein C deficiency is started on warfarin. Three days later she develops sharply demarcated, dusky areas of dying skin over her thighs, and biopsy shows thrombosed small vessels. Which of the following best explains this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An early fall in proteins C and S produces a transient hypercoagulable state

choices.label: B
choices.text: Warfarin directly damages the vascular endothelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibodies to platelet factor 4 activate platelets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excessive inhibition of factor VII causes bleeding into the skin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Accumulation of vitamin K promotes small-vessel clotting

explanation.summary: Warfarin-induced skin necrosis results from an early drop in the short-lived anticoagulant proteins C and S, causing transient hypercoagulability.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that warfarin inhibits synthesis of the anticoagulant proteins C and S, which have short half-lives, so early warfarin therapy can cause transient hypercoagulability. This can thrombose small vessels and kill skin (warfarin-induced skin necrosis). Patients with hereditary protein C or S deficiency are particularly susceptible because their anticoagulant protein levels are already low.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Warfarin does not directly damage endothelium; the necrosis is caused by transient hypercoagulability from falling proteins C and S.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antibodies to platelet factor 4 describe the mechanism of HITT with heparin, not warfarin-induced skin necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Skin necrosis is due to clotting from hypercoagulability, not bleeding from factor VII inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin blocks the use of vitamin K rather than causing its accumulation, and vitamin K excess does not cause skin necrosis.

questions.stem: Warfarin reduces production of clotting factors in both the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways, yet it is monitored primarily with the prothrombin time (PT) and INR. Which of the following best explains why the PT/INR is the earliest indicator of warfarin effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Factor VII has the shortest half-life of the vitamin K-dependent factors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Factor II has the longest half-life of the vitamin K-dependent factors

choices.label: C
choices.text: The PT reflects only the intrinsic pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warfarin has no effect on the common pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: The PTT is unaffected by warfarin

explanation.summary: Factor VII of the extrinsic pathway has the shortest half-life, so the PT/INR (which monitors the extrinsic pathway) rises first.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that warfarin is mainly monitored with the PT or INR because factor VII, part of the extrinsic pathway, has the shortest half-life of all the vitamin K-dependent factors. Because the PT monitors the extrinsic pathway, warfarin prolongs the PT/INR before the PTT, making the PT/INR an earlier indication of warfarin effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The relevant point is factor VII's short half-life, not factor II having the longest half-life.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The PT mainly monitors the extrinsic and common pathways, not the intrinsic pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Warfarin does affect common pathway factors such as II and X, so this is incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin does prolong the PTT as well; it simply prolongs the PT/INR earlier.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man on warfarin presents with severe, life-threatening gastrointestinal bleeding and a markedly elevated INR. Which of the following is the most effective agent to reverse the cause of his bleeding?

explanation.summary: Prothrombin complex concentrate replaces the factors warfarin depletes and is the most effective reversal for severe warfarin-related bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that PCC essentially replaces the clotting factors warfarin depletes (factors II, VII, IX, and X, plus proteins C and S) to reverse the cause of bleeding. It is preferred over fresh frozen plasma, which is less effective and used only when PCC is unavailable; PCC and vitamin K are often given together.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Protamine sulfate is the antidote for heparin, not warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Idarucizumab specifically reverses dabigatran, not warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tranexamic acid is an antidote for thrombolytic drugs and does not replace warfarin-depleted factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin K alone will not reverse severe bleeding quickly; it repletes vitamin K to prevent further bleeding but works slowly given warfarin's long half-life.

questions.stem: A patient stable on warfarin is started on fluconazole, an inhibitor of CYP2C9. If not adjusted, this change is most likely to produce which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A supratherapeutic INR with increased risk of bleeding

choices.label: B
choices.text: A subtherapeutic INR with increased risk of clotting

choices.label: C
choices.text: No change in INR because warfarin is renally cleared

choices.label: D
choices.text: A shortened PT due to faster factor synthesis

explanation.summary: CYP2C9 inhibitors reduce warfarin metabolism, raising warfarin levels and prolonging the INR, which increases bleeding risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that warfarin metabolism is primarily hepatic by CYP2C9, and starting a CYP2C9 inhibitor can result in a prolonged or supratherapeutic INR, increasing the risk of bleeding. Fluconazole is named as one such inhibitor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A subtherapeutic INR would result from increased vitamin K intake or enzyme induction, not from a CYP2C9 inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Warfarin is metabolized hepatically by CYP2C9, so an inhibitor does change the INR.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting metabolism raises warfarin levels and prolongs, not shortens, the PT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is a complication of heparin, not of a warfarin-CYP2C9 interaction.

questions.stem: A patient with acute venous thromboembolism is treated with heparin. Heparin exerts its anticoagulant effect by activating antithrombin, which then directly inhibits which of the following clotting factors?

explanation.summary: Heparin activates antithrombin, which inhibits factors Xa and IIa (thrombin) in the common pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that the various forms of heparin act indirectly by activating the endogenous anticoagulant antithrombin, and antithrombin activation then inhibits clotting factors Xa and IIa (thrombin), both in the common pathway.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Factors V and VIII are cofactors in the cascade and are not the factors directly inhibited by antithrombin here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factors VII and IX belong to the extrinsic and intrinsic pathways and are not the common-pathway targets described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factors I (fibrinogen) and XIII act in the final steps of clot formation and stabilization, not the antithrombin target step.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factors IX and X are part of the intrinsic and common pathways, but the specific antithrombin-mediated targets emphasized are Xa and IIa.

questions.stem: A patient receiving unfractionated heparin for deep vein thrombosis develops heavy gastrointestinal bleeding with hypotension. Which of the following would best reverse the action of heparin in this patient?

explanation.summary: Protamine sulfate is the antidote for heparin, binding and inactivating it.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that abnormal bleeding from heparin products can be treated with the antidote protamine sulfate, a positively charged molecule that binds to negatively charged heparin and inactivates it.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vitamin K is an antidote for warfarin, not heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fresh frozen plasma is used for warfarin-related bleeding when PCC is unavailable, not as the heparin antidote.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tranexamic acid is an antidote for thrombolytic drugs, not heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antithrombin is the anticoagulant that heparin activates; giving it would not reverse heparin.

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient on unfractionated heparin develops a falling platelet count and a new deep vein thrombosis. Testing confirms heparin-induced thrombocytopenia and thrombosis (HITT). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in anticoagulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Switch to a direct thrombin inhibitor such as argatroban

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase the dose of unfractionated heparin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer protamine sulfate and continue heparin

explanation.summary: When HITT is suspected, heparin must be stopped and the patient switched to a direct thrombin inhibitor.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that HITT is an autoimmune reaction in which heparin binds platelet factor 4, and the heparin-PF4-antibody triad activates platelets, causing thrombosis and thrombocytopenia. When HITT is suspected the patient needs to be switched immediately to a direct thrombin inhibitor, which is the anticoagulant of choice; argatroban is one such agent.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Increasing heparin worsens the reaction, since heparin is the trigger for HITT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low-molecular-weight heparin is still a heparin product and is not the appropriate choice in HITT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Protamine reverses heparin bleeding but does not treat HITT, and continuing heparin is contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin alone is not appropriate acute management for HITT; a direct thrombin inhibitor is the anticoagulant of choice.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl is evaluated for lifelong easy bruising, frequent nosebleeds, and heavy menstrual periods. Physical exam shows scattered petechiae. A complete blood count reveals a low platelet count, and the peripheral smear shows strikingly large (giant) platelets. Platelet aggregation studies are normal in response to ADP, collagen, and epinephrine but show no response to ristocetin. Which of the following is the most likely defective protein?

explanation.summary: Giant platelets with thrombocytopenia and an absent ristocetin response indicate Bernard-Soulier syndrome, a defect in glycoprotein Ib.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bernard-Soulier syndrome is caused by a defect in the glycoprotein Ib (GPIb) receptor, which normally binds von Willebrand factor to anchor platelets to the exposed subendothelium. Because ristocetin tests GPIb-mediated adhesion, the aggregation curve to ristocetin is absent, while responses to ADP, collagen, and epinephrine are preserved. The platelets are characteristically giant and the platelet count is often low.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A defect in GPIIb/IIIa causes Glanzmann thrombasthenia, in which the platelet count and platelet appearance are normal and ristocetin aggregation is preserved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Loss of alpha granules produces gray platelet syndrome, whose platelets appear empty/gray rather than simply giant, and ristocetin response is not the defining abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Delta-granule deficiency shows histologically normal platelets on smear, not giant platelets, and requires electron microscopy to detect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The problem is the platelet receptor for von Willebrand factor, not von Willebrand factor itself; vWF levels are not the primary defect in this inherited platelet disorder.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old man has a history of severe mucocutaneous bleeding since childhood. His platelet count and platelet morphology on peripheral smear are normal. Aggregation studies show no aggregation in response to ADP, collagen, epinephrine, or arachidonic acid, but the response to high-dose ristocetin is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Absent aggregation to all agonists except a preserved ristocetin response, with normal platelet count and appearance, is characteristic of Glanzmann thrombasthenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a defect of the GPIIb/IIIa receptor, which binds fibrinogen to bridge platelets during aggregation. As a result there is no aggregation to ADP, collagen, epinephrine, or arachidonic acid, while ristocetin (which depends on the intact GPIb-vWF pathway) remains normal. Platelet count and morphology are normal because this is an aggregation problem, not an adhesion or granule problem.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bernard-Soulier syndrome shows the opposite aggregation pattern (absent ristocetin) plus giant platelets and a low platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gray platelet syndrome shows large, gray, empty platelets from absent alpha granules and often thrombocytopenia, not this aggregation pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Delta-granule deficiency involves impaired dense-granule secretion and is diagnosed by electron microscopy, not this classic loss of all agonist responses with preserved ristocetin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemophilia A is a clotting-factor deficiency causing deep tissue and joint bleeding with a prolonged PTT, not a platelet aggregation defect.

questions.stem: During normal platelet plug formation, dense (delta) granules release small molecules that are essential for platelet activation. Which of the following pairs of substances is released primarily by delta granules?

explanation.summary: Delta (dense) granules release calcium and serotonin, small molecules essential for platelet activation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes that delta granules release small molecules such as calcium and serotonin, which are essential for platelet activation, whereas alpha granules release larger proteins. Loss of these granules underlies delta-granule deficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrinogen and von Willebrand factor are released from alpha granules, not delta granules.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Collagen is a subendothelial protein and thrombin is generated in the coagulation cascade; neither is a delta-granule content.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heparin and histamine are associated with mast cells and basophils, not platelet dense granules.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Factors VIII and IX are plasma coagulation factors, not substances stored in platelet dense granules.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman has easy bruising and mild thrombocytopenia. Her peripheral smear shows large platelets that appear pale, empty, and gray compared with normally pigmented platelets. Additional evaluation reveals myelofibrosis, splenomegaly, and an elevated serum vitamin B12 level. Which of the following best explains the abnormal platelet appearance?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduction or absence of alpha granules

explanation.summary: Large, empty, gray platelets with thrombocytopenia, myelofibrosis, and elevated B12 describe gray platelet syndrome, caused by loss of alpha granules.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gray platelet syndrome is defined by a reduction or complete absence of alpha granules. Without granule contents, the giant platelets look empty or gray on the smear. Other features described in the brick include progressive thrombocytopenia, myelofibrosis, splenomegaly, and elevated serum B12.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A GPIb defect causes Bernard-Soulier syndrome, whose giant platelets are not described as empty/gray and which lacks the myelofibrosis and elevated B12 picture.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dense (delta) granule deficiency produces histologically normal-appearing platelets requiring electron microscopy, not gray empty platelets.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A GPIIb/IIIa defect causes Glanzmann thrombasthenia, with normal platelet count and appearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: von Willebrand factor deficiency is von Willebrand disease, which does not produce empty gray platelets on the smear.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl has a history of easy bruising and recurrent bacterial infections; a maternal uncle had a bleeding disorder. Her complete blood count shows a low platelet count, and the platelets appear normal on the peripheral smear. PT and PTT are normal, but the PFA-100 is abnormal. A combined bleeding and immune deficiency such as Chediak-Higashi syndrome is suspected. Which of the following studies is most likely to confirm the platelet abnormality?

explanation.summary: Delta-granule deficiency has normal light-microscopy platelets, so electron microscopy is needed to detect the abnormal granules.
explanation.whyCorrect: Delta-granule deficiency is frequently found together with disorders such as Chediak-Higashi or Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, producing combined bleeding and immunodeficiency. Because the platelets look normal on routine smear, the brick states that electron microscopy is used to detect the abnormal dense granules.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A routine light-microscopy smear appears normal in delta-granule deficiency, so repeating it will not reveal the defect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PT/INR reflects the coagulation cascade, which is normal in platelet disorders and does not identify a granule defect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron studies address iron deficiency, a supportive management issue, not the diagnosis of a granule abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Karyotype analysis does not visualize platelet granules and is not the test used to confirm delta-granule deficiency.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy with suspected hereditary platelet dysfunction undergoes laboratory testing. Which of the following sets of results would be most consistent with an inherited platelet disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged PT, prolonged PTT, normal platelet function test

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal PT, normal PTT, abnormal platelet function test

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prolonged PT, normal PTT, normal platelet function test

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normal PT, prolonged PTT, normal platelet function test

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prolonged PT, prolonged PTT, abnormal platelet function test

explanation.summary: In platelet disorders the coagulation cascade is intact, so PT and PTT are normal while the platelet function test is abnormal.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because hereditary platelet disorders affect the platelets and not the coagulation cascade, the PT and PTT are normal. The platelet function analyzer (PFA-100), which has largely replaced the bleeding time, is abnormal, reflecting impaired platelet function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A prolonged PT and PTT with a normal platelet function test points to a coagulation-factor problem, not a platelet disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A prolonged PT alone suggests a factor deficiency in the extrinsic pathway, not impaired platelet function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A prolonged PTT alone suggests an intrinsic-pathway factor deficiency such as hemophilia, not a platelet disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet disorders do not prolong PT or PTT, so a picture with both prolonged is inconsistent with an isolated platelet defect.

questions.stem: A physician is teaching students about how hereditary platelet disorders present clinically. Which of the following bleeding patterns is LEAST consistent with an inherited platelet disorder and should instead raise concern for a clotting-factor deficiency such as hemophilia?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bleeding into joints and deep muscles

explanation.summary: Platelet disorders cause mucocutaneous bleeding; deep tissue and joint bleeding instead suggests a clotting-factor deficiency like hemophilia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that bleeding involving deep tissues (muscles) or joints is not commonly associated with platelet disorders and is more typical of clotting-factor deficiencies such as hemophilia. Hereditary platelet disorders instead cause mucocutaneous bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Petechiae are small pinpoint capillary breaks seen on the skin and are a classic mucocutaneous sign of platelet disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nosebleeds (epistaxis) are a common mucocutaneous manifestation of platelet dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gum bleeding on mucosal surfaces is typical mucocutaneous bleeding seen in platelet disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Purpura and ecchymoses are cutaneous bleeding lesions characteristic of platelet disorders.

questions.stem: A man with Glanzmann thrombasthenia and his wife, whose genetic background is unknown, ask about the chance their child will be affected. Both Glanzmann thrombasthenia and Bernard-Soulier syndrome follow which of the following inheritance patterns?

explanation.summary: Bernard-Soulier syndrome and Glanzmann thrombasthenia are both autosomal recessive disorders.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explicitly states that both Bernard-Soulier syndrome and Glanzmann thrombasthenia are autosomal recessive. Two copies of the affected gene are required to produce disease, so an affected father must pass his gene and the mother must also be a carrier for the child to be affected.

whyWrongByChoice.A: These receptor disorders are recessive, not dominant; a single copy of the gene does not produce the syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: They are autosomal, not X-linked recessive. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome is an example of an X-linked disorder, but it is a different condition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The inheritance is recessive and autosomal, not X-linked dominant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: These disorders result from nuclear gene defects in platelet glycoproteins, not mitochondrial inheritance.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman with Bernard-Soulier syndrome has recurrent bleeding episodes that are difficult to control. In addition to supportive measures, which of the following targeted therapies is most appropriate for this disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A thrombopoietin agonist such as eltrombopag

explanation.summary: Bernard-Soulier syndrome can be treated with recombinant factor VIIa or a thrombopoietin agonist such as eltrombopag.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists recombinant factor VIIa or a thrombopoietin agonist (eltrombopag) as specific treatments for Bernard-Soulier syndrome. Thrombopoietin agonists can raise the platelet count and help control bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Warfarin is an anticoagulant that would worsen bleeding in a patient with a hereditary platelet disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin impairs platelet function and is avoided (like NSAIDs) in these patients, not used as therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IVIG is used for immune thrombocytopenia, not for the inherited receptor defect of Bernard-Soulier syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Corticosteroids treat immune-mediated platelet destruction, but Bernard-Soulier syndrome is an inherited structural receptor defect.

questions.stem: A patient with a hereditary platelet disorder has received numerous platelet transfusions over several years to control bleeding, but recent transfusions have become progressively less effective. Which of the following best explains this loss of effectiveness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of antiplatelet and HLA alloantibodies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Depletion of the patient's own clotting factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of iron overload in the bone marrow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Conversion of the disorder to acute leukemia

explanation.summary: Repeated platelet transfusions can induce antiplatelet and HLA antibodies that limit the effectiveness of subsequent transfusions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick warns that repeated transfusions may lead to formation of antiplatelet antibodies (especially against platelet glycoproteins) and HLA antibodies. These alloantibodies limit the usefulness of later transfusions by targeting the transfused platelets.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Clotting-factor depletion is not the mechanism; platelet disorders leave the coagulation cascade intact and transfusions supply platelets, not factors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vitamin K deficiency affects clotting factors and PT, not the effectiveness of platelet transfusions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron overload is a concern with chronic red-cell transfusion but does not explain refractoriness to platelet transfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hereditary platelet disorders do not transform into acute leukemia as a result of transfusion.

questions.stem: A hematologist wants to order the single best laboratory test for assessing overall platelet function in a patient with suspected hereditary platelet dysfunction. Which of the following tests has largely replaced the bleeding time for this purpose?

explanation.summary: The PFA-100 (platelet function analyzer) has largely replaced the bleeding time as the best test of platelet function.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that patients with hereditary platelet disorders will have an abnormal platelet function test, such as the PFA-100, which has largely replaced the bleeding time as the best test for platelet function.

whyWrongByChoice.B: PT measures the extrinsic coagulation pathway, not platelet function, and is normal in platelet disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.C: D-dimer reflects fibrin degradation and is used to evaluate thrombosis, not primary platelet function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrinogen level measures a clotting protein, not the functional capacity of platelets.
