whyWrongByChoice.E: PTT measures the intrinsic coagulation pathway and is normal in platelet disorders.

questions.stem: A newborn with Glanzmann thrombasthenia is noted to have leukocytosis, delayed separation of the umbilical cord, and a severe bacterial infection. These additional findings are best explained by co-occurrence of which of the following conditions?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin K deficiency of the newborn

explanation.summary: In infants, Glanzmann thrombasthenia can co-occur with leukocyte adhesion deficiency, explaining leukocytosis, delayed cord separation, and severe infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that Glanzmann thrombasthenia in infants often presents at birth with leukocytosis, delayed umbilical cord separation, or severe bacterial infections because the disorder often co-occurs with a leukocyte adhesion deficiency, a type of immunodeficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron deficiency anemia causes fatigue and microcytic anemia, not leukocytosis with delayed cord separation and severe infection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy with bleeding and clotting, not the inherited leukocyte defect described here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K deficiency causes bleeding from low clotting factors but does not cause leukocytosis or delayed umbilical cord separation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hereditary spherocytosis is a red-cell membrane disorder causing hemolytic anemia, unrelated to these leukocyte and infection findings.

questions.stem: An asymptomatic 30-year-old man has a routine complete blood count performed at a wellness visit. His hematologist notes that in adults the normal platelet count ranges from 150,000 to 400,000 cells/uL. Below which of the following platelet counts is a patient, by definition, considered to have thrombocytopenia?

explanation.summary: Thrombocytopenia is defined as a platelet count below 150,000 cells/uL, the lower limit of the normal adult range.
explanation.whyCorrect: The normal adult platelet count is 150,000 to 400,000 cells/uL, so any count below 150,000 cells/uL meets the definition of thrombocytopenia. Lower thresholds mark increasing severity but are not the defining cutoff.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 400,000 cells/uL is the upper limit of the normal range, not a threshold for thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 100,000 cells/uL is below normal but is not the definitional cutoff; a count below 150,000 already qualifies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Below 50,000 cells/uL marks the point at which serious bleeding risk and skin signs appear, but it is not the definition of thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Below 10,000 cells/uL is a hematologic emergency with risk of spontaneous bleeding, not the definitional threshold.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and COPD is admitted for pneumonia and subsequently develops a deep venous thrombosis. She is started on several medications. Five days after admission, a complete blood count shows that her platelet count has dropped from 250,000 to 70,000 cells/uL. Which of the following medications is the most likely cause of her thrombocytopenia?

explanation.summary: A significant platelet drop 5-10 days after starting heparin for a venous thrombosis is classic for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) develops when a circulating antibody complexes heparin with platelet factor 4, leading to platelet destruction and paradoxical thrombosis. It typically occurs 5-10 days after heparin exposure, which matches this patient treated for a DVT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol is used for COPD and is not an established cause of thrombocytopenia in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Azithromycin treats the pneumonia; drug-induced thrombocytopenia from it is far less likely than from heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lisinopril is an outpatient antihypertensive and is not a typical cause of acute thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetaminophen is not a recognized cause of the acute platelet drop seen here.

questions.stem: A medical student is asked to describe the skin findings of a patient with thrombocytopenia. Both petechiae and purpura are present. Which of the following best differentiates petechiae from purpura?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Petechiae appear on the abdomen while purpura appears only on the legs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Petechiae are more numerous than purpura

choices.label: C
choices.text: Petechiae are red while purpura are purple

choices.label: D
choices.text: Petechiae are caused by inflammation while purpura are caused by bleeding

explanation.summary: Petechiae and purpura are distinguished chiefly by size: petechiae are less than 3 mm, whereas purpura are 3 to 10 mm.
explanation.whyCorrect: Petechiae are nonblanchable superficial macules smaller than 3 mm, while purpura are larger lesions of 3 to 10 mm. Both result from extravasation of blood from small vessels into the skin, so size is the key distinguishing feature.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Location does not differentiate the two; both can occur at the same sites.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Relative number is not a defining distinction between petechiae and purpura.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Color overlaps (both are red to purple) and is not the reliable distinguishing feature.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both petechiae and purpura share the same etiology of subcutaneous bleeding, not inflammation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man receiving chemotherapy has a platelet count of 8,000 cells/uL. He has no bruising, petechiae, or active bleeding, and platelet clumping has been excluded. According to standard practice, prophylactic platelet transfusion is generally indicated once the platelet count falls below which of the following values in a non-bleeding patient?

explanation.summary: In a patient who is not actively bleeding, prophylactic platelet transfusion is generally given once the count is below 10,000 cells/uL.
explanation.whyCorrect: A platelet count below 10,000 cells/uL carries a high risk of spontaneous bleeding, so patients at or below this level should receive transfusion even without active bleeding. Actively bleeding patients with low counts are also transfused, with a goal above 50,000 cells/uL for most patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Above 100,000 cells/uL is a post-transfusion goal for patients bleeding into the central nervous system, not a prophylactic transfusion threshold.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 50,000 cells/uL is the general post-transfusion goal and the surgical safety threshold, not the prophylactic trigger.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 30,000 cells/uL is not the standard prophylactic transfusion threshold for a non-bleeding patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 5,000 cells/uL is lower than the accepted trigger; transfusion is indicated before the count falls this far.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has a complete blood count as part of a pre-employment physical. The CBC is normal except for a platelet count of 8,000 cells/uL. She feels well, and no bruises, petechiae, or purpura are found on examination. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in working up her thrombocytopenia?

explanation.summary: A markedly low platelet count in a completely asymptomatic patient suggests artifactual (pseudo)thrombocytopenia, which is investigated first with a peripheral blood smear.
explanation.whyCorrect: Such a low count with no bleeding signs is suspicious for pseudothrombocytopenia from platelet clumping in the EDTA specimen. A peripheral smear can reveal the clumps; if present, a repeat CBC in a citrate tube gives an accurate count. Pseudothrombocytopenia should be excluded before further workup or treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bone marrow biopsy is a last resort, generally reserved for confirmed low counts with pancytopenia, not for a possible lab artifact.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Coagulation studies are part of the broader workup but should follow confirmation that the platelet count is truly low.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Liver function tests are useful once real thrombocytopenia is established, not as the immediate next step.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal function tests belong to the later workup and do not address the likely clumping artifact.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man with infectious mononucleosis is found to have a platelet count of 95,000 cells/uL. He has no bleeding symptoms, and his hemoglobin and white blood cell count are normal. On examination he has a palpably enlarged spleen. Which of the following mechanisms best explains his thrombocytopenia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immune destruction of platelets by autoantibodies

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bone marrow infiltration by leukemic cells

explanation.summary: An enlarged spleen from mononucleosis sequesters an increased fraction of platelets, producing mild thrombocytopenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally about one-third of platelets are sequestered in the splenic sinusoids. When the spleen enlarges (hypersplenism), a greater proportion is trapped there and unavailable to the circulation, lowering the measured platelet count. Infection such as mononucleosis is a recognized cause of splenomegaly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immune destruction (ITP) is not indicated here; the splenomegaly points to sequestration rather than autoantibody-mediated destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no pancytopenia or abnormal cells to suggest marrow infiltration by leukemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no heparin exposure, so HIT antibodies are not the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There are no schistocytes or coagulation abnormalities to indicate consumption within microthrombi.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman develops petechiae on her lower legs and gum bleeding two weeks after a viral upper respiratory illness. She feels otherwise well. Her CBC shows an isolated platelet count of 18,000 cells/uL with a normal hemoglobin and white blood cell count. The peripheral smear shows reduced but otherwise normal-appearing platelets and no schistocytes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Isolated thrombocytopenia in an otherwise well patient, often after a viral illness, is characteristic of immune thrombocytopenia (ITP).
explanation.whyCorrect: ITP is a common acquired autoimmune cause of thrombocytopenia in which autoantibodies destroy platelets in the spleen. It typically presents as isolated thrombocytopenia with normal other cell lines and can follow infections; the smear shows reduced but morphologically normal platelets.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DIC shows schistocytes, prolonged PT/PTT, and a critically ill patient, none of which are present here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HIT requires recent heparin exposure and characteristically causes thrombosis, not this post-viral isolated thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Marrow suppression from leukemia would typically cause pancytopenia with abnormal cells, not isolated thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypersplenism would be expected to produce splenomegaly and often affects multiple cell lines rather than isolated platelets.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man in the ICU with gram-negative sepsis develops oozing from his intravenous catheter sites and around his gums. Laboratory studies show a platelet count of 40,000 cells/uL, prolonged prothrombin time and partial thromboplastin time, and schistocytes on the peripheral smear. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his thrombocytopenia?

explanation.summary: Sepsis with bleeding, thrombocytopenia, prolonged coagulation times, and schistocytes indicates disseminated intravascular coagulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DIC, derangement of the coagulation cascade drives rapid platelet consumption as microthrombi form, producing thrombocytopenia. The associated microangiopathic damage generates schistocytes, and simultaneous consumption of clotting factors prolongs both the PT and PTT. Prolonged coagulation studies are the exception in thrombocytopenia and point specifically to DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia with normal PT/PTT and no schistocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a hereditary platelet aggregation defect with a normal platelet count, not consumptive thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pseudothrombocytopenia is a lab artifact in a well patient and would not cause real bleeding or abnormal coagulation studies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HIT characteristically produces thrombosis rather than diffuse bleeding, and it does not typically prolong the PT/PTT or produce schistocytes.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 35 weeks of gestation presents with right upper quadrant pain and malaise. Laboratory studies show a platelet count of 60,000 cells/uL, elevated liver transaminases, and evidence of hemolysis on the peripheral smear. Which of the following best explains her thrombocytopenia?

explanation.summary: Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets in a pregnant woman define HELLP syndrome, a microangiopathic consumptive process.
explanation.whyCorrect: HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets) occurs in pregnancy and causes rapid platelet consumption within microthrombi, a form of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. The combination of pregnancy, right upper quadrant pain, transaminase elevation, and hemolysis is characteristic.

whyWrongByChoice.B: ITP produces isolated thrombocytopenia without hemolysis or elevated liver enzymes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TTP is a thrombotic microangiopathy but classically presents with neurologic changes and renal involvement rather than the liver-enzyme elevation of pregnancy-related HELLP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIT requires heparin exposure and causes thrombosis, not the hemolysis and liver findings seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Splenic sequestration causes mild thrombocytopenia from an enlarged spleen and does not explain hemolysis with elevated liver enzymes.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man reports several weeks of fatigue and is noted to be pale. His CBC shows a platelet count of 35,000 cells/uL, a hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, and a white blood cell count of 2,000/uL. The peripheral smear shows abnormal-appearing cells across multiple cell lines. Platelet clumping has been excluded. Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repeat CBC in a citrate tube

explanation.summary: Pancytopenia with abnormal cells on smear indicates possible marrow failure or neoplasia, which is evaluated with a bone marrow biopsy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The concurrent presence of thrombocytopenia, anemia, and leukopenia defines pancytopenia and points to pathology in the bone marrow. A bone marrow biopsy is indicated to distinguish marrow toxicity from hematopoietic neoplasia and to identify a neoplasm such as leukemia if present.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Clumping has already been excluded, so a citrate-tube recount for pseudothrombocytopenia is unnecessary.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coagulation studies do not explain pancytopenia or identify a marrow disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transfusion may be needed for symptoms but is not a diagnostic step and does not establish the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal function tests would not explain pancytopenia with abnormal cells across cell lines.

questions.stem: A hematologist is teaching students how the bleeding pattern of thrombocytopenia differs from that of a coagulation-factor disorder such as hemophilia. Which of the following findings most strongly suggests a platelet problem (thrombocytopenia) rather than a coagulation-factor disorder?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed bleeding that begins hours after injury

choices.label: D
choices.text: A prolonged PTT with a normal platelet count

explanation.summary: Thrombocytopenia causes superficial mucocutaneous bleeding with petechiae, unlike the deep tissue bleeding of coagulation-factor disorders.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bleeding in thrombocytopenia is superficial, presenting as petechiae, purpura, and mucous membrane bleeding from the gums and nose. This contrasts with hemophilia and other coagulation disorders, in which bleeding is deep, such as into joints and muscles.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hemarthrosis is a hallmark of coagulation-factor disorders such as hemophilia, not thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Delayed bleeding hours after injury is typical of coagulation-factor defects rather than platelet problems.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A prolonged PTT with a normal platelet count reflects a coagulation-cascade abnormality, not thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Deep intramuscular hematomas indicate a coagulation-factor disorder, not a platelet disorder.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man treated with heparin for 6 days develops a 60% drop in his platelet count along with a new arterial clot. A heparin-PF4 antibody test is positive. Heparin is stopped immediately. Because he still requires anticoagulation for his thrombosis, which of the following is the most appropriate next agent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Restart heparin at a lower dose

explanation.summary: After stopping heparin in HIT, anticoagulation is continued with an alternative agent such as the direct thrombin inhibitor argatroban.
explanation.whyCorrect: In heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, stopping heparin is the first step, and the next is starting an alternative anticoagulant. Direct thrombin inhibitors such as argatroban or bivalirudin, or direct factor Xa inhibitors such as rivaroxaban or apixaban, are appropriate choices to treat the ongoing thrombotic risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Restarting heparin would reintroduce the offending agent and worsen the antibody-mediated process.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Warfarin should not be started alone in acute HIT because of thrombotic risk; a nonheparin anticoagulant is used first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent and provides inadequate anticoagulation for an active HIT-related thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet transfusion can fuel further thrombosis in HIT and does not address the anticoagulation need.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is diagnosed with immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). Her antiplatelet antibodies have bound to glycoproteins on the surface of her platelets. Which of the following best explains how these antibody-coated platelets are removed from the circulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activation of complement causes intravascular lysis of the platelets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrophages in the spleen phagocytose the platelets via Fc receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Natural killer cells recognize and destroy the platelets

choices.label: D
choices.text: The bone marrow stops releasing platelets into the blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: The liver sequesters and stores the platelets long-term

explanation.summary: In ITP, IgG-opsonized platelets are cleared by splenic macrophages through Fc receptor binding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antibodies (usually IgG) coat the platelets in a process called opsonization. Macrophages in the spleen then bind the Fc portion of these antibodies via their Fc receptors, phagocytose the antibody-coated platelets, and remove them completely from the circulation, lowering the platelet count.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The brick attributes platelet loss to splenic phagocytosis, not to complement-mediated intravascular lysis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Destruction is carried out by splenic macrophages, not natural killer cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Marrow production is actually increased in ITP (increased megakaryocytes); platelets are destroyed peripherally, not held back by the marrow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: It is splenic macrophages that engulf the platelets; the liver does not simply store them.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with immune thrombocytopenia undergoes a bone marrow biopsy as part of an atypical workup. Which of the following findings would most likely be seen in her marrow?

explanation.summary: ITP marrow shows increased megakaryocytes because platelet destruction is peripheral, not a production problem.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because platelets are destroyed peripherally in the spleen, the marrow compensates by making more, so it shows increased megakaryocytes. A bone marrow biopsy is not required to diagnose ITP.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Megakaryocytes are increased, not absent; production is intact and even ramped up.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Blast infiltration suggests leukemia, not ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Marrow fibrosis is a feature of myelofibrosis, not ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The marrow is not hypocellular; the megakaryocyte line is expanded.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with isolated thrombocytopenia is suspected of having immune thrombocytopenia. Aside from the reduced number of platelets, which of the following best describes her peripheral blood smear?

explanation.summary: ITP shows isolated thrombocytopenia; platelets are normal or occasionally large, with normal red and white cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The smear in ITP demonstrates isolated thrombocytopenia: platelets are decreased in number while red and white blood cells look normal. The platelets themselves appear normal, though some may be larger than normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Leukocytosis with blasts suggests leukemia, not the isolated thrombocytopenia of ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Red cell anisocytosis points to an anemia (e.g., iron deficiency), not ITP, in which red cells are normal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Schistocytes indicate a microangiopathic process, not ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gray, agranular platelets suggest gray platelet syndrome, a hereditary disorder, not ITP.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is being evaluated for a low platelet count, and immune thrombocytopenia is being considered. Which of the following additional findings, if present, would most strongly argue against a diagnosis of ITP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concurrent anemia and leukopenia on the complete blood count

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased megakaryocytes on bone marrow examination

choices.label: D
choices.text: A normal prothrombin time and partial thromboplastin time

choices.label: E
choices.text: A recent viral upper respiratory infection

explanation.summary: ITP requires an isolated thrombocytopenia; involvement of other cell lines points away from ITP.
explanation.whyCorrect: ITP is diagnosed only when the thrombocytopenia is isolated, meaning the red and white blood cells are normal in number. Coexisting anemia and leukopenia mean the thrombocytopenia is not isolated, so another cause (such as a marrow or systemic disorder) is more likely.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased megakaryocytes are the expected marrow finding in ITP and support, rather than exclude, the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Petechiae are a typical presenting sign of ITP and are consistent with the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Normal PT and PTT are expected, since ITP is a platelet problem, not a coagulation factor problem.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A recent viral infection is a recognized secondary trigger of ITP and does not argue against it.

questions.stem: A clinician evaluating a patient with suspected immune thrombocytopenia considers ordering an antiplatelet antibody test to confirm the diagnosis. Which of the following is the best reason this test is not relied upon for diagnosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It detects only IgM antibodies, which are not involved in ITP

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is positive in nearly all healthy people

choices.label: D
choices.text: It requires a bone marrow biopsy to perform

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is prohibitively expensive and rarely available

explanation.summary: Antiplatelet antibody tests are not used because their sensitivity is low.
explanation.whyCorrect: The antibody tests have a very low sensitivity: about half of patients with ITP have a negative result. This makes the test unreliable for confirming the diagnosis, which is why ITP remains a clinical diagnosis of exclusion.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The antibodies in ITP are usually IgG, and the problem with the test is poor sensitivity, not the antibody class it detects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick cites low sensitivity (false negatives), not universal positivity in healthy people.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The antibody test does not require a bone marrow biopsy; marrow biopsy is not needed for ITP at all.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cost and availability are not the reasons given; the issue is the test's low sensitivity.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic with the sudden appearance of a petechial rash and bruising two weeks after a viral upper respiratory infection. He otherwise looks and feels well, and his labs show an isolated thrombocytopenia. Which of the following is the most likely course of his condition?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It will require urgent splenectomy to prevent death

choices.label: C
choices.text: It typically progresses to acute leukemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a lifelong chronic disease requiring continuous therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: It usually causes fatal intracerebral hemorrhage within days

explanation.summary: Acute ITP in children follows a viral illness, is self-limited, and usually resolves within months.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute ITP is most common in children and usually follows a viral illness. Although the onset is abrupt, the child typically looks and feels well, the disease is self-limited, generally requires no treatment, and most children recover completely within a few months.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Splenectomy is a third-line therapy reserved for refractory chronic ITP, not a routine step in self-limited childhood ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute ITP is self-limited and does not progress to leukemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A lifelong chronic course is characteristic of chronic ITP in adults, not acute childhood ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Life-threatening bleeding such as intracerebral hemorrhage is rare; the child usually looks and feels well.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with petechiae and an isolated thrombocytopenia and is diagnosed with chronic immune thrombocytopenia. Which of the following statements best characterizes chronic ITP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is most common in adult females younger than 40

choices.label: B
choices.text: It occurs exclusively in children following a viral illness

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is always self-limited and resolves within a few weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: It nearly always presents with life-threatening bleeding

choices.label: E
choices.text: It results from decreased production of megakaryocytes

explanation.summary: Chronic ITP most often affects adult women under 40 and may be primary or secondary to disorders like lupus, HIV, or CLL.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic ITP presents most commonly in adult females younger than 40 years. It may be primary or secondary, occurring in the setting of conditions such as lupus, HIV infection, or chronic lymphocytic leukemia, with a gradual onset and common recurrences.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Occurring in children after a viral illness describes acute ITP, not chronic ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A brief, self-limited course is characteristic of acute childhood ITP; chronic ITP tends to recur.
whyWrongByChoice.D: In most chronic ITP, bleeding is relatively minor, and life-threatening bleeding is rare.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ITP is caused by peripheral antibody-mediated destruction, and megakaryocytes are actually increased.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old man develops isolated thrombocytopenia and petechiae. His history is notable for a chronic viral infection, and his clinician suspects secondary immune thrombocytopenia due to molecular mimicry. Which of the following is a recognized secondary cause of ITP by this mechanism?

explanation.summary: Viral infections such as hepatitis C can cause secondary ITP through molecular mimicry.
explanation.whyCorrect: In viral illness, antibodies generated to fight the virus cross-react with the patient's platelets, a process called molecular mimicry. Cited examples include HIV, hepatitis C, CMV, varicella, and coronavirus.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron deficiency causes anemia, not antibody-mediated platelet destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypothyroidism is not listed as a secondary cause of ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Factor V Leiden is an inherited thrombophilia, unrelated to antiplatelet antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and is not a molecular-mimicry trigger for ITP.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with symptomatic chronic immune thrombocytopenia and a very low platelet count is started on treatment. Which of the following is the usual first-line therapy?

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids such as prednisone are the usual first-line therapy for ITP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The goal of treatment is to keep the platelet count at a safe level to prevent severe bleeding. Corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) are the usual first-line therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Rituximab is typically the first choice for relapse after a steroid taper, not initial therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Splenectomy is now a third-line option reserved for corticosteroid failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TPO agonists are used for recurrence after a steroid taper, not as first-line treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet transfusions are generally avoided because the transfused platelets are destroyed just as quickly.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with immune thrombocytopenia has a severely low platelet count and active bleeding, and her platelet count must be raised quickly. Which of the following treatments is most appropriate to rapidly raise her platelet count?

explanation.summary: IVIG rapidly raises the platelet count in ITP, though its effect is transient.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) is used to rapidly raise the platelet count, although the effect is transient. It is useful in the setting of significant bleeding or as pre-operative treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Corticosteroids are first-line therapy but do not raise the platelet count as rapidly as IVIG.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rituximab is used for relapse after steroid taper and does not act rapidly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Splenectomy is a third-line surgical option, not a rapid pharmacologic measure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TPO agonists are used for recurrence and act by stimulating production over time, not rapidly.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with refractory immune thrombocytopenia that has failed medical therapy is scheduled for splenectomy. Which of the following is most important to do before the procedure?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transfuse platelets to achieve a normal count

explanation.summary: Patients undergoing splenectomy must be immunized against encapsulated bacteria beforehand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Splenectomy removes the site of platelet destruction and can be effective, but because the spleen is important for defense against encapsulated organisms, patients must be immunized against encapsulated bacteria before the procedure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Transfused platelets are destroyed as fast as the patient's own, so routine transfusion to a normal count is not the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ITP is a bleeding disorder; anticoagulation with warfarin would be harmful and is not indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron supplementation treats iron deficiency anemia and has no role in preparing for splenectomy in ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bone marrow transplant is not part of ITP management; marrow production is already intact.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman with immune thrombocytopenia and mild petechiae asks why she cannot simply receive a platelet transfusion to normalize her count. Which of the following best explains why platelet transfusions are generally avoided in ITP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The transfused platelets are destroyed just as rapidly as the patient's own platelets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transfused platelets reliably trigger fatal anaphylaxis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transfusion permanently increases antiplatelet antibody production

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transfusion transmits the virus that caused the ITP

explanation.summary: Platelet transfusions are avoided in ITP because the transfused platelets are destroyed as fast as the patient's own.
explanation.whyCorrect: In ITP, the antiplatelet antibodies destroy transfused donor platelets just as fast as the patient's own platelets, so transfusion is generally avoided. It may be used only in patients with severe thrombocytopenia and significant bleeding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anaphylaxis is not the reason transfusions are avoided in ITP; rapid destruction of the platelets is.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick does not describe transfusion as permanently boosting antibody production.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron overload is a concern with chronic red cell transfusion, not the reason platelets are avoided in ITP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transfusion is avoided because the platelets are quickly destroyed, not because it transmits a causative virus.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man who underwent a bone marrow transplant 10 days ago has thrombocytopenia. His platelet count was 120,000/mm3 on Monday and 100,000/mm3 on Wednesday, and it continues to fall. Below which of the following platelet counts is he most likely to develop spontaneous bleeding?

explanation.summary: Spontaneous (non-traumatic) bleeding generally does not occur until the platelet count drops below 10,000/mm3.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick's platelet count table shows that the risk of spontaneous bleeding appears only when counts fall below 10,000/mm3. Above that level, bleeding, if any, is usually provoked by trauma rather than occurring spontaneously.

whyWrongByChoice.B: At counts around 20,000-50,000/mm3 a patient may bleed more after injuries, but spontaneous bleeding is not expected.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A count of 60,000/mm3 is not usually associated with significant bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A count of 80,000/mm3 is above the threshold for any abnormal bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A count of 100,000/mm3 is only mild thrombocytopenia and is not associated with significant bleeding.

questions.stem: A sample of platelet-rich plasma is sent to the laboratory for aggregation studies. When exposed to ADP, collagen, epinephrine, and ristocetin, the platelets aggregate normally in response to every agent. Which of the following conclusions is best supported by these results?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient has abnormal platelet function

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sample contains a sufficient quantity of platelets

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient is able to form a normal fibrin clot

explanation.summary: Aggregation to all agonists indicates normal platelet function; the test does not assess platelet quantity or fibrin formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normal platelets respond to every aggregating agent, so aggregation with ADP, collagen, epinephrine, and ristocetin is considered normal platelet function. Abnormal responses to one or more agents would instead suggest a functional defect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Abnormal function would show diminished aggregation to one or more agents, which is not the case here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aggregation studies measure how platelets respond, not how many platelets are present.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bernard-Soulier would show absent aggregation to ristocetin, so a normal ristocetin response excludes it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet aggregation studies assess primary hemostasis and do not evaluate fibrin (secondary hemostasis).

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman is referred after continuous oozing from wisdom-tooth extraction sites. Her prothrombin time/INR, partial thromboplastin time, and platelet count are all within normal limits. Which of the following tests is the preferred next step to evaluate how well her platelets function?

explanation.summary: The PFA-100 is the preferred test of platelet function, assessing how well the platelet plug forms.
explanation.whyCorrect: When counts and coagulation studies are normal but bleeding persists, a platelet function problem is suspected. The PFA-100 is the preferred assay of platelet function; blood is drawn through a standardized aperture and the machine measures plug formation, detecting abnormal adhesion and aggregation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The platelet count is already normal and does not indicate how well the platelets work.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The prothrombin time evaluates the coagulation cascade, not platelet function, and is already normal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Morphology can reveal size/shape abnormalities but most diseases have normal-appearing platelets and it does not measure function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemoglobin electrophoresis evaluates hemoglobin types and is unrelated to platelet function.

questions.stem: During a platelet aggregation study, a patient's platelets aggregate normally in response to ADP, collagen, and epinephrine but fail to aggregate in response to ristocetin. Genetic and receptor testing shows the platelets lack the normal glycoprotein Ib (GpIb) receptor for von Willebrand factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Bernard-Soulier syndrome is caused by a lack of the platelet GpIb receptor for vWF, producing absent ristocetin-induced aggregation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ristocetin works by driving vWF (GpIb) receptors to the platelet surface so they can bind vWF. In Bernard-Soulier syndrome the platelets lack the normal GpIb receptor, so no matter how much ristocetin is added the platelets will not aggregate, while responses to other agonists remain normal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombocytosis is a high platelet count and does not cause an isolated loss of ristocetin response.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune thrombocytopenia lowers the platelet count rather than removing the GpIb receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K deficiency impairs the coagulation cascade, not platelet ristocetin aggregation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemophilia A is a factor VIII deficiency of secondary hemostasis and does not affect platelet aggregation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with lifelong easy bruising and heavy menses undergoes aggregation testing. Her platelets aggregate normally to ADP, collagen, and epinephrine but fail to aggregate with ristocetin. Repeat testing after mixing her platelets with normal von Willebrand factor restores the ristocetin response. Which of the following best explains her original result?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Absence of the platelet GpIb receptor

choices.label: D
choices.text: An elevated platelet count causing sludging

explanation.summary: In von Willebrand disease the platelets are normal but there is no functional vWF, so ristocetin-induced aggregation is absent.
explanation.whyCorrect: In von Willebrand disease the platelets express normal vWF (GpIb) receptors, but there is no normal von Willebrand factor for them to bind, so they do not aggregate with ristocetin. Supplying normal vWF corrects the defect, distinguishing it from Bernard-Soulier syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: If the GpIb receptor itself were absent (Bernard-Soulier), adding normal vWF would not restore aggregation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor VIII deficiency affects the coagulation cascade, not ristocetin-induced platelet aggregation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A high platelet count causes sludging/ischemia, not a selective loss of the ristocetin response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Absent granules is a different morphologic defect and would not be corrected by adding vWF.

questions.stem: A hematology technician reports that an automated analyzer produced a platelet count that appears falsely low. Small clumps of platelets were noted when the specimen was checked. Which of the following is the most appropriate way to obtain an accurate platelet count?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Measure the von Willebrand factor level

explanation.summary: When platelet clumps or other small cells cause a falsely altered automated count, the count should be verified manually on a smear.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although modern analyzers usually give reliable counts, platelet clumps or misread small cells can falsely elevate or decrease the automated result. In these instances the platelet count must be performed manually by examining a blood smear under the microscope.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The PFA-100 measures platelet function, not the number of platelets.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The bleeding time assesses function of primary hemostasis, not the platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ristocetin aggregation testing evaluates platelet response to vWF, not quantity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A vWF assay measures von Willebrand factor level and does not quantify platelets.

questions.stem: A medical student asks why the platelet function assay (PFA-100) has largely replaced the older bleeding time test. Which of the following is the best explanation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bleeding time is difficult to standardize

choices.label: B
choices.text: The bleeding time evaluates the coagulation cascade rather than platelets

choices.label: C
choices.text: The bleeding time cannot be performed when the platelet count is normal

choices.label: D
choices.text: The bleeding time measures fibrin formation directly

choices.label: E
choices.text: The bleeding time requires platelet-rich plasma and aggregating agents

explanation.summary: The bleeding time is hard to standardize, which is why the PFA-100 is replacing it.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bleeding time involves piercing the forearm with a lancet and recording how long the cut bleeds. It is being replaced by the PFA-100 because it is difficult to standardize, with variables such as the size of the lancet, the skin depth and durability, and the technician's blotting technique.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Both the bleeding time and PFA-100 assess platelet-driven primary hemostasis, not the coagulation cascade.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The bleeding time evaluates function and can be done with a normal platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neither the bleeding time nor the PFA-100 reflects fibrin formation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platelet-rich plasma and aggregating agents describe aggregation studies, not the bleeding time.

questions.stem: A patient with abnormal bleeding has a PFA-100 result showing decreased platelet function and a bleeding time that is prolonged. The clinician notes that neither of these tests reveals the underlying cause of the platelet dysfunction. Which of the following tests would best help determine the specific cause?

explanation.summary: Neither the PFA-100 nor the bleeding time explains why platelets malfunction; aggregation studies with specific agents narrow the cause.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PFA-100 and bleeding time show that platelets are working poorly but not why. Determining the cause is done using platelet aggregation studies that show the response to specific factors, since the pattern of response to different agents helps narrow the diagnosis (for example, Bernard-Soulier or von Willebrand disease).

whyWrongByChoice.B: A platelet count gives quantity, not the cause of dysfunction, and is often normal in these patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Repeating the bleeding time would only reconfirm the functional defect without revealing its cause.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The prothrombin time evaluates the coagulation cascade, not the cause of platelet dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mean platelet volume reflects platelet size, which does not necessarily reflect function or its cause.

questions.stem: A physician wants to assess how well a patient can form a clot before major elective surgery. Which of the following statements about the platelet count is most accurate in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A normal platelet count does not indicate that the platelets function normally

choices.label: B
choices.text: A normal platelet count confirms that platelet function is normal

choices.label: C
choices.text: The platelet count directly measures platelet aggregation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The platelet count evaluates the coagulation cascade

choices.label: E
choices.text: A normal platelet count rules out von Willebrand disease

explanation.summary: The platelet count reflects quantity, not function; a patient can bleed abnormally with a normal count.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick emphasizes that the platelet count does not indicate platelet function. A patient can have a bleeding disorder and still have a normal platelet count, which is why functional tests such as the PFA-100 and aggregation studies are needed to evaluate clotting ability.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A normal count does not confirm normal function; function requires separate testing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aggregation is measured by aggregation studies, not by the platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The coagulation cascade is assessed by tests such as PT and PTT, not the platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Von Willebrand disease can be present despite a normal platelet count.

questions.stem: A blood smear from a patient with a suspected hereditary platelet disorder shows unusually large platelets, and some platelets appear gray because they lack granules. Which of the following best describes the general clinical utility of assessing platelet morphology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is used less often than the platelet count

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the preferred method to measure platelet function

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can be reliably performed by automated analyzers alone

choices.label: D
choices.text: It directly measures the response of platelets to aggregating agents

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is the initial step in evaluating any abnormal bleeding

explanation.summary: Morphology is used less frequently than the count because most diseases show normal-appearing platelets, though it is key in certain hereditary disorders.
explanation.whyCorrect: Assessing morphology requires manual smear review and is used less frequently than the total platelet count because most diseases, even those involving platelets, have normal-appearing platelets. In certain conditions, however, such as hereditary platelet disorders with giant platelets or gray platelets lacking granules, morphology is an important part of the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The preferred measure of platelet function is the PFA-100, not morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Morphology requires manual microscopic evaluation because autoanalyzers cannot detect subtle abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Response to aggregating agents is measured by aggregation studies, not by morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Measuring the platelet count, not morphology, is the initial step in evaluating abnormal bleeding.

questions.stem: In a platelet aggregation study, an aggregating agent is added to a tube of platelet-rich plasma. As the reaction proceeds, the plasma becomes less cloudy (less turbid). Which of the following does this decrease in turbidity most directly indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The platelets are aggregating in response to that agent

choices.label: B
choices.text: The platelet count in the sample is falling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrin is being formed in the tube

choices.label: D
choices.text: The platelets are failing to respond to that agent

choices.label: E
choices.text: Von Willebrand factor is being consumed

explanation.summary: In aggregation studies, platelets clumping together clears the plasma, so decreasing turbidity indicates aggregation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The principle of the test is that adding an aggregating agent to platelet-rich plasma causes platelets to clump together, which makes the plasma less cloudy or turbid. A decrease in turbidity therefore means the platelets are aggregating properly in response to that particular agent.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The clearing reflects platelets clumping, not a change in the total number of platelets present.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aggregation studies assess platelet clumping, not fibrin formation (secondary hemostasis).
whyWrongByChoice.D: A failure to respond would leave the plasma cloudy, not make it clearer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The turbidity change tracks platelet aggregation, not consumption of von Willebrand factor.

questions.stem: A patient with abnormal bleeding has normal aggregation to all agonists except ristocetin, to which the platelets do not respond. To distinguish von Willebrand disease from Bernard-Soulier syndrome, the clinician orders a von Willebrand factor assay (von Willebrand antigen). Which of the following results would best support a diagnosis of von Willebrand disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A low von Willebrand factor level

choices.label: B
choices.text: A normal von Willebrand factor level

explanation.summary: The vWF assay measures the antigen level directly, and the level is low in von Willebrand disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both von Willebrand disease and Bernard-Soulier syndrome show absent ristocetin aggregation. A specialized von Willebrand factor assay (von Willebrand antigen) measures the vWF level directly, and this level will be low in von Willebrand disease, whereas in Bernard-Soulier the defect is the missing platelet receptor and vWF is present.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A normal vWF level would point away from von Willebrand disease and toward a platelet receptor defect such as Bernard-Soulier.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The platelet count is not the distinguishing feature between these two disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrin formation is part of secondary hemostasis and is not measured by the vWF assay.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mean platelet volume reflects platelet size and does not establish a low vWF level.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with several days of bloody diarrhea, vomiting, and decreased urination. Her family lives in a community where romaine lettuce was recently recalled. Her complete blood count shows anemia and thrombocytopenia, and the peripheral smear shows numerous schistocytes. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shiga toxin from Escherichia coli O157:H7

choices.label: C
choices.text: An autoantibody against a phospholipid-binding protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: A defect in complement regulatory factors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Overactivation of the coagulation cascade from sepsis

explanation.summary: Diarrhea-associated HUS in a young child is most often caused by Shiga toxin from E. coli O157:H7.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most common type of HUS is acquired and associated with an infectious diarrheal illness due to a toxin-producing gut bacterium. The most common HUS-causing toxin is Shiga toxin, usually from E. coli O157:H7, which is absorbed through the gastrointestinal mucosa and alters endothelial cells, leading to platelet activation and microthrombi.

whyWrongByChoice.B: ADAMTS13 deficiency causes TTP, which is seen mostly in young adults and characteristically involves neurologic findings rather than a preceding diarrheal illness.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antibodies against a phospholipid-binding protein describe antiphospholipid syndrome, not diarrhea-associated HUS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A complement factor defect causes only a rare genetic form of HUS, not the common diarrhea-associated form suggested by the lettuce recall and bloody diarrhea.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Overactivation of the coagulation cascade describes DIC, which would prolong the PT and aPTT and typically occurs with systemic infection or trauma.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who was previously healthy presents with fever, confusion, and easy bruising. Laboratory studies show anemia, a low platelet count, schistocytes on the smear, and a mildly elevated creatinine. Which of the following features most strongly favors thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura over hemolytic-uremic syndrome?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schistocytes on the peripheral blood smear

explanation.summary: Neurologic involvement is characteristic of TTP and is unusual in HUS.
explanation.whyCorrect: In TTP, but not HUS, the brain may be involved because of microthrombi leading to strokes and cerebral ischemia, producing confusion, headache, dysarthria, seizures, or coma. HUS is characterized by the same findings as TTP except that neurologic findings are unusual and fever is less common.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Schistocytes reflect microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and are present in both TTP and HUS, so they do not distinguish the two.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombocytopenia occurs in both TTP and HUS from platelet consumption and is not distinguishing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia is a shared feature of all thrombotic microangiopathies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A preceding diarrheal illness points toward HUS rather than TTP.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is diagnosed with thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. Her disease stems from deficiency of the enzyme ADAMTS13. Which of the following best describes the direct consequence of this enzyme deficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers are not cleaved

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fibrin is deposited excessively because the coagulation cascade is overactivated

choices.label: C
choices.text: Red blood cells are destroyed by autoantibodies directed against membrane antigens

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complement is activated in an unregulated fashion, injuring endothelium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Platelet production in the bone marrow is impaired

explanation.summary: ADAMTS13 normally cleaves ultra-large vWF multimers; its deficiency lets platelets adhere to these multimers and form thrombi.
explanation.whyCorrect: ADAMTS13 normally inhibits thrombosis by cleaving ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers into smaller multimers that are less likely to form clots. In TTP, ADAMTS13 deficiency prevents this cleavage, so circulating platelets adhere readily to the large multimers, leading to formation of thrombi in small blood vessels.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Excess fibrin from an overactivated coagulation cascade describes DIC; TTP thrombi are platelet-rich with minimal fibrin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TTP does not involve autoantibodies against red cell membrane antigens; RBC damage is mechanical, from shearing by microthrombi.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Unregulated complement activation describes the rare genetic form of HUS, not TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombocytopenia in TTP results from platelet consumption in clots, not impaired marrow production.

questions.stem: A hematologist reviews the peripheral blood smear of a patient with suspected thrombotic microangiopathy and identifies fragmented red cells that are small, lack central pallor, and have pointy edges. Which of the following statements about these cells is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are specific for microangiopathic hemolytic anemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are frequently artifactual and should be disregarded

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are nuclear remnants seen in patients with asplenia

choices.label: D
choices.text: They indicate a coexisting glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are a normal finding when the bone marrow is healthy

explanation.summary: Schistocytes are specific for MAHA and never appear on a normal or artifactual smear.
explanation.whyCorrect: When RBCs travel through small vessels, they are sheared by microthrombi, producing fragmented cells called schistocytes. They are identifiable by their small size, lack of central pallor, and pointy edges, and are specific to MAHA; schistocytes never appear on a normal blood smear and are never artifactual.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Schistocytes are never artifactual; their presence is a genuine and meaningful finding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nuclear remnants are Howell-Jolly bodies seen with asplenia, not schistocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bite cells, not schistocytes, are associated with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Schistocytes are never seen on a normal smear, regardless of marrow health.

questions.stem: A patient with thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura has widespread microthrombi and a very low platelet count, yet the prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) are normal. Which of the following best explains these normal coagulation results?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The thrombi are composed largely of platelets with minimal fibrin

choices.label: B
choices.text: The coagulation cascade is overactivated, rapidly consuming clotting factors

choices.label: C
choices.text: The liver compensates by increasing synthesis of all clotting factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antibodies inhibit the thromboplastin reagent used in the assay

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vitamin K deficiency prevents activation of the assay

explanation.summary: In TTP and HUS the thrombi are platelet-rich with minimal fibrin, so coagulation factors are not consumed and the PT and aPTT stay normal.
explanation.whyCorrect: In TTP, HUS, and most secondary causes of TM, the intravascular thrombi result from platelet aggregation rather than activation of the coagulation cascade. The thrombi are composed largely of platelets with minimal fibrin, so coagulation factors are not excessively consumed, and the PT and aPTT are not prolonged.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Overactivation of the cascade with factor consumption occurs in DIC, where the PT and aPTT become elevated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Normal coagulation times reflect that factors are not consumed, not increased hepatic synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reagent inhibition by antibodies describes the prolonged aPTT of antiphospholipid syndrome, not the normal values in TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin K deficiency would prolong, not normalize, the coagulation studies and is unrelated to TTP.

questions.stem: An adult woman is admitted with fever, disorientation, petechiae, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and schistocytes on the peripheral smear. Coagulation studies are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for her condition?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Supportive care with intravenous fluids only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fresh frozen plasma to replace consumed clotting factors

explanation.summary: TTP is a medical emergency treated with urgent plasmapheresis, often with corticosteroids.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has TTP, which is a medical emergency managed with urgent plasmapheresis (plasma exchange) to clear and replace the defective ADAMTS13. Systemic corticosteroids are used alongside plasmapheresis, and drugs such as rituximab may also be used.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Antibiotics are not useful in most cases of thrombotic microangiopathy, including TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Supportive care with fluids is the mainstay for HUS, not the definitive treatment for TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet transfusion is not the treatment for TTP and does not address the underlying ADAMTS13 problem.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fresh frozen plasma replaces clotting factors for bleeding from factor consumption, which is not the mechanism in TTP; plasma exchange is required.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy develops hemolytic-uremic syndrome after an episode of bloody diarrhea. He has electrolyte abnormalities and acute kidney injury. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of his condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supportive care with correction of electrolytes and intravenous fluids

choices.label: E
choices.text: High-dose antibiotics to eradicate the causative bacterium

explanation.summary: HUS is managed primarily with supportive care, including fluids, electrolyte correction, and renal support.
explanation.whyCorrect: HUS treatment is primarily supportive care. Because most patients present with diarrhea, electrolyte abnormalities, and AKI, management means correcting electrolytes, supporting renal function, and providing intravenous fluids as needed. Antibiotics are not useful in most cases.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Urgent plasmapheresis with corticosteroids is the treatment for TTP, not the primarily supportive approach to HUS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Long-term anticoagulation is not the management of HUS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Splenectomy has no role in the treatment of HUS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antibiotics are not useful in most cases of HUS and are not the mainstay of therapy.

questions.stem: A patient with a thrombotic microangiopathy has laboratory studies performed to characterize the hemolysis. Which of the following combinations of serum findings is most consistent with the intravascular hemolytic anemia of this disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High unconjugated bilirubin, high LDH, and low haptoglobin

choices.label: B
choices.text: High conjugated bilirubin, low LDH, and high haptoglobin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low LDH, low reticulocyte count, and high haptoglobin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normal bilirubin, normal LDH, and normal haptoglobin

choices.label: E
choices.text: High haptoglobin, low bilirubin, and low reticulocyte count

explanation.summary: Intravascular hemolysis produces high unconjugated bilirubin, increased LDH, and low haptoglobin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Serum studies in TM are typical of intravascular hemolytic anemia, showing high unconjugated bilirubin, increased LDH, and low haptoglobin because of RBC lysis. Reticulocytes also rise as the marrow tries to make more RBCs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemolysis raises unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin and LDH and lowers haptoglobin, the opposite of this option.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low LDH and high haptoglobin argue against hemolysis, and the reticulocyte count typically rises.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Entirely normal values would not reflect the active intravascular hemolysis of a thrombotic microangiopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High haptoglobin and low bilirubin are inconsistent with hemolysis; haptoglobin falls and bilirubin rises.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman at 34 weeks of gestation presents with right upper quadrant abdominal pain and hypertension. Laboratory studies show hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, a low platelet count, and schistocytes on the smear. Which of the following secondary causes of thrombotic microangiopathy best explains her presentation?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Malignancy with micrometastases to vessel walls

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome is a pregnancy-associated secondary TM with hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets.
explanation.whyCorrect: HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets) is a secondary cause of TM in which pregnant patients develop MAHA and low platelets similar to TTP, causing abdominal pain and hypertension. It can progress to placental abruption and organ failure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DIC would prolong the PT and aPTT and usually arises with systemic infection or trauma rather than the pregnancy-specific picture described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stem cell transplantation causes TM through associated radiation therapy or chemotherapy, which does not fit a pregnant patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calcineurin inhibitors cause drug-induced TM through direct endothelial injury and are used for transplant rejection, not in this scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Malignancy-associated TM results from micrometastases to vessel walls and is not suggested by this obstetric presentation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a blood pressure of 230/125 mm Hg. Laboratory studies reveal anemia with schistocytes, thrombocytopenia, and a rising serum creatinine. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for his hematologic and renal findings?

choices.label: E
choices.text: March hemolysis from repetitive foot impact

explanation.summary: Malignant hypertension is a secondary TM that can cause MAHA, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Malignant hypertension, defined as a blood pressure above 220/100 mm Hg, is a secondary cause of TM that can cause vessel injury, often leading to MAHA, low platelets, and AKI with a rising serum creatinine. Secondary causes such as this should be considered and excluded before diagnosing HUS or TTP.

whyWrongByChoice.B: ADAMTS13 deficiency causes TTP, which is not defined by severe hypertension and is a primary rather than secondary TM.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Shiga toxin causes diarrhea-associated HUS in young children, not a hypertensive emergency in an older adult.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A complement factor defect causes a rare genetic form of HUS, which does not explain the markedly elevated blood pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: March hemolysis is a rare cause of MAHA from RBC fragmentation in the soles of the feet and does not involve severe hypertension or AKI.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman is diagnosed with acquired thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. Which of the following is the most common mechanism responsible for the ADAMTS13 deficiency in her form of the disease?

choices.label: B
choices.text: An inactivating mutation in the ADAMTS13 gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: Consumption of ADAMTS13 by widespread fibrin thrombi

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased hepatic synthesis from vitamin K deficiency

explanation.summary: Acquired TTP is most often caused by an autoantibody that inhibits ADAMTS13.
explanation.whyCorrect: The deficiency of ADAMTS13 can be inherited or acquired. Acquired cases, which are more common, mostly result from an autoantibody directed against ADAMTS13. Some acquired cases occur late in pregnancy, when ADAMTS13 levels drop.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An inactivating gene mutation causes the inherited form of TTP, which is less common than the acquired autoantibody-mediated form.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TTP thrombi are platelet-rich with minimal fibrin, and the enzyme deficiency is not due to consumption by fibrin thrombi.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Shiga toxin causes HUS and has no role in the ADAMTS13 deficiency of TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin K deficiency affects clotting factor synthesis, not ADAMTS13 levels.

questions.stem: A patient with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and schistocytes has an uncertain diagnosis after routine testing, and the clinician wants to confirm immune thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. A special assay is ordered. Which of the following results would best confirm this diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ADAMTS13 activity under 10% with a detectable antibody inhibitor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Schistocytes and a low platelet count on the peripheral smear

explanation.summary: Immune TTP is confirmed by ADAMTS13 activity under 10% and/or a detectable inhibitor against ADAMTS13.
explanation.whyCorrect: When the cause of a suspected TM remains unclear, special assays can confirm TTP from ADAMTS13 deficiency. These suggest immune TTP if the ADAMTS13 activity is under 10% and/or an antibody inhibitor against ADAMTS13 is detected. Because results may take several days, treatment should not be delayed to wait for them.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An elevated D-dimer is nonspecific and elevated in most forms of thrombosis, so it does not confirm TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A prolonged PT points toward DIC; in TTP the PT is characteristically normal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrin degradation products are elevated in many thrombotic states and do not confirm a diagnosis of TTP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Schistocytes and thrombocytopenia are seen in all thrombotic microangiopathies and cannot by themselves distinguish immune TTP.
